proposals.title: Learning to be Surprised: A Self-Supervised Feature Space for Intrinsic Reward
proposals.motivation: In reinforcement learning, curiosity-driven exploration using prediction error in a learned feature space is a powerful paradigm. However, pre-2026 methods often rely on generic self-supervised objectives (e.g., contrastive learning) that may not yield features optimal for measuring meaningful, task-relevant surprise. This leads to inefficient exploration, as agents can be distracted by stochastic noise or predictable but irrelevant dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a feature space explicitly optimized to maximize the information gain from prediction errors will yield a more robust and efficient intrinsic reward signal for exploration than features learned for generic representation tasks.
proposals.method: We will train a world model with a transformer-based persistent state, inspired by recent 3D perception models. The core innovation is a dual-objective training loop. While the primary forward dynamics model is trained to minimize prediction error, a secondary 'meta-predictor' network will be trained to classify, from the feature space, whether a given transition was predictable or novel. The feature encoder will be adversarially optimized to 'fool' this meta-predictor, forcing it to learn features where errors are maximally informative. This draws inspiration from counterfactual reasoning methods like Visual Jenga to isolate object-dependent surprises.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, principled method for learning curiosity-optimized representations. We expect to demonstrate more sample-efficient exploration in visually complex 3D environments and a theoretical framework linking feature space properties to information-theoretic exploration. The resulting architecture could serve as a drop-in module for existing world model-based agents.
proposals.novelty: Instead of using off-the-shelf self-supervised features, we propose to directly optimize the feature space for the downstream goal of generating intrinsic reward, using a novel adversarial meta-learning objective. This moves beyond treating the feature space as a static preprocessing step.

proposals.title: PixelSeed: Generative Scene Reconstruction from a Single Pixel via Persistent State and Counterfactual Attention
proposals.motivation: Current inpainting and image completion models require substantial context or large masked regions, limiting their ability to test fundamental scene understanding. This work asks: what is the minimal visual seed from which a coherent scene can be generated? Answering this probes the model's internal world model and its ability to hallucinate plausible, consistent scenes from near-zero information, a critical test for generative vision systems.
proposals.hypothesis: A vision transformer with a persistent latent state, trained with a counterfactual attention mechanism, can generate diverse, globally coherent images from a single pixel by treating the seed as a constraint that propagates through iterative refinement.
proposals.method: We will adapt a Vision Transformer (ViT) architecture with a persistent memory bank, inspired by 'Continuous 3D Perception' work, to maintain scene consistency. Training will use a novel 'Visual Jenga'-inspired objective: given a single pixel from a real image, the model must generate the full image, while a discriminator evaluates global coherence. We will introduce a 'counterfactual attention' layer where the model attends to the seed pixel's feature against learned 'register' tokens (from 'Vision Transformers Don't Need Trained Registers') to hypothesize and diffuse plausible scene structures.
proposals.contribution: This will introduce a new, extreme benchmark for generative scene understanding. It will yield a method to visualize and analyze the associative priors embedded in large-scale vision models, showing how local cues trigger global scene hypotheses. The persistent state mechanism will provide insights into iterative image formation.
proposals.novelty: Pushing inpainting to the absolute limit of a one-pixel seed is unexplored. The combination of a persistent state model with counterfactual reasoning for this task moves beyond standard autoregressive or diffusion approaches, framing generation as a constrained hypothesis-testing process.

proposals.title: Inside-Out: Probing Scene Understanding by Predicting Context from a Central Token
proposals.motivation: Current vision models excel at tasks like inpainting and object recognition, which focus on inferring local content from global context. This proposal inverts the standard paradigm to test a deeper, more causal form of scene understanding. By requiring a model to predict the plausible surroundings given only a central region, we directly assess its grasp of object dependencies, physical constraints, and scene semantics, moving beyond pattern completion to model-based reasoning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model trained for this 'inside-out' prediction task will develop a more structured and persistent internal representation of scenes, capturing latent factors like object affordances and physical laws, which will transfer robustly to downstream tasks requiring compositional reasoning.
proposals.method: We will build on the Visual Jenga counterfactual framework and persistent state models. A Vision Transformer (ViT) will be trained with a novel attention masking strategy: only tokens from a variable-sized central image patch are visible as input, while the model must autoregressively predict the surrounding token sequence. We will experiment with training regimes, including a two-stage process with an initial masked autoencoding phase, and test time adaptation using the model's persistent memory to refine predictions.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel diagnostic task and benchmark for evaluating scene understanding. It will yield models with improved compositional generalization and a methodology for extracting interpretable, object-centric latent variables from the model's attention patterns and persistent state.
proposals.novelty: While inpainting predicts the missing from the known, this inverts the core conditioning, making it a fundamentally different and more constrained generative task. It leverages the 'registers' or persistent memory from recent Efros-group work not as a training crutch, but as a core mechanism for maintaining a consistent scene hypothesis.

proposals.title: Contextual Jigsaw: Learning Visual Representations by Predicting Surrounding Token Statistics
proposals.motivation: Unsupervised pre-training for vision has been dominated by masked image modeling (MIM), which excels at low-level texture synthesis but may under-emphasize global scene structure. Inpainting, as explored in Visual Jenga, is one such MIM variant. A complementary class of tasks—predicting broader context from a local observation—could encourage models to learn more about object relationships and scene layout, which is crucial for high-level vision tasks. This remains underexplored compared to generative inpainting objectives.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that training a vision transformer to predict statistical summaries of a surrounding context region from a central image patch will yield visual representations that are more sensitive to scene structure and object dependencies than standard masked autoencoding, while remaining competitive on downstream classification and detection.
proposals.method: We will design a novel pre-training task where, given a central image patch (e.g., 25% of the image), the model must predict a set of lightweight statistical 'context tokens' summarizing the surrounding area. These tokens will encode features like the histogram of semantic class logits from a frozen teacher network, dominant color and texture statistics, and a coarse spatial layout signature. The model, a standard ViT, will process the central patch via its self-attention layers, and a small prediction head will output the context token sequence. Training will use a regression loss (e.g., smooth L1) between predicted and ground-truth context tokens, avoiding pixel-level reconstruction.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce and systematically evaluate a new family of context prediction tasks for unsupervised visual representation learning. We will provide a detailed analysis comparing the learned features to those from inpainting-based methods on benchmarks like ImageNet classification and COCO object detection. The resulting 'Contextual Jigsaw' task could become a standard alternative or complement to MIM in self-supervised learning pipelines.
proposals.novelty: While predicting missing parts (inpainting) is common, predicting aggregated *descriptions* of the missing context from a visible seed is novel. This shifts the focus from pixel synthesis to relational and statistical reasoning, directly building on the 'object dependencies' theme of Visual Jenga but through a discriminative, non-generative objective.

proposals.title: Spatiotemporal Context Encoders: Self-Supervised Feature Learning via Video Inpainting
proposals.motivation: Current self-supervised learning for video often relies on simple temporal pretext tasks like frame order prediction or contrastive learning across frames, which may not capture complex, long-range spatiotemporal structure. Video inpainting—synthesizing missing content across both space and time—is a challenging, high-level task that could force a model to learn rich, coherent representations of object dynamics and scene semantics, but it has been underexplored as a self-supervised objective. This work aims to bridge this gap, leveraging the power of context encoding for holistic video understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that training a model to perform long-range, spatiotemporally coherent video inpainting will yield visual representations that outperform those from existing pretext tasks on downstream video understanding benchmarks, particularly for tasks requiring reasoning about object interactions and scene dynamics.
proposals.method: We will extend the image-based context encoder framework to video by designing a 3D Vision Transformer (ViT) architecture with spatiotemporal attention. The model will be trained to reconstruct large, randomly masked 3D volumes (blocks of frames and pixels) from raw video data. Inspired by 'Visual Jenga', we will strategically mask object regions across multiple frames to encourage learning of object dependencies and persistent state. Training will leverage efficient attention mechanisms, potentially incorporating ideas from untrained register tokens to manage sequence length, and will be evaluated by transferring frozen features to action recognition, video object segmentation, and novel 'counterfactual' video prediction tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a novel self-supervised objective (video inpainting) for spatiotemporal feature learning, 2) a transformer-based architecture optimized for long-range video context modeling, and 3) new insights into what visual and temporal structures models learn by solving the inpainting task, contributing to both representation learning and generative video modeling.
proposals.novelty: While image inpainting is a known pretext task, its systematic extension to *video* for self-supervised *feature learning* is novel. The key innovation is framing video inpainting not as a pure generative outcome, but as a mechanism to discover object and scene dynamics, directly building on Efros's prior work in image context and counterfactual reasoning.

proposals.title: Scaling the Persistent State: Evaluating 3D Feature Learning on Large-Scale Pre-training for Transfer
proposals.motivation: Recent vision models, including Vision Transformers (ViTs) and persistent-state 3D models, are typically pre-trained on curated 2D image datasets like ImageNet. However, the feature learning capabilities and transfer performance of models with explicit 3D inductive biases, such as those with a persistent scene state, remain untested on the scale and diversity of modern web-scale datasets (e.g., LAION). This gap is critical because scaling data is known to improve 2D representation quality, but its effect on 3D-aware feature learning for downstream tasks like object manipulation or scene understanding is unknown.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pre-training a 3D perception model with a persistent state on a large-scale, diverse dataset will yield visual features that significantly outperform current 2D and smaller-scale 3D pre-trained models on a broad suite of transfer tasks, particularly those requiring geometric and physical reasoning.
proposals.method: We will scale up the pre-training of a model based on the 'Continuous 3D Perception Model with Persistent State' architecture. Using a dataset on the scale of LAION-5B, we will employ a masked autoencoding objective while maintaining the model's persistent 3D latent scene representation across sequences of web images. We will rigorously benchmark the learned features against state-of-the-art 2D ViTs (e.g., DINOv2, MAE) and the original model on controlled transfer tasks, including fine-grained classification, semantic segmentation, and—crucially—counterfactual reasoning tasks inspired by 'Visual Jenga' to probe physical and dependency understanding.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first large-scale empirical evidence for the data scalability of 3D-aware persistent representation learning. It will establish new baselines for transfer learning with geometrically grounded features and could reveal the data regimes where 3D inductive biases become most beneficial. The resulting model and benchmarks will be a valuable resource for the community.
proposals.novelty: While scaling 2D pre-training is common, applying this paradigm to a model class designed for persistent 3D state is novel. The evaluation will uniquely connect web-scale pre-training to performance on counterfactual physical reasoning tasks, moving beyond standard classification benchmarks.

proposals.title: MegaPaint: Efficient High-Resolution Inpainting via Hierarchical Latent Diffusion and Persistent State
proposals.motivation: Current inpainting models struggle with megapixel images due to the quadratic memory cost of global attention in transformers and the computational burden of direct pixel-space diffusion. This limits practical applications in high-resolution photo editing, medical imaging, and large-scale scene completion. Adapting these models for very high resolution is a critical, unsolved scalability challenge in computer vision.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hierarchical latent diffusion model, combined with a persistent, coarse-to-fine scene state, can achieve coherent and efficient inpainting in megapixel images by enforcing long-range consistency without global self-attention over all high-resolution tokens.
proposals.method: We will adapt a latent diffusion architecture, operating in a compressed VQ-GAN latent space to reduce dimensionality. Inspired by 'Continuous 3D Perception Model with Persistent State', we will maintain a persistent, low-resolution global feature map that is updated over time to guide the inpainting process. For local coherence, we will use efficient attention mechanisms like shifted window attention (Swin Transformer) or axial attention within local patches, avoiding full self-attention on the full megapixel latent grid. Training will leverage a curriculum strategy, starting with lower resolutions and progressively increasing image size.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical model for high-fidelity, high-resolution inpainting, pushing the boundary of generative models for large images. It will contribute a novel architectural design that decouples global scene reasoning from local texture synthesis for scalability. The findings will also provide insights into managing long-range dependencies in vision transformers under extreme memory constraints.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of a persistent, evolving scene state from 3D perception models into a 2D inpainting pipeline to maintain global consistency, bypassing the need for prohibitively expensive global attention on megapixel latents. This approach is distinct from simply scaling up existing patch-based or convolutional inpainting methods.

proposals.title: Inverse Scene Completion: A Diffusion-Based Framework for Globally Consistent Multi-Region Inpainting
proposals.motivation: Current inpainting methods excel at filling single, localized holes but struggle with multiple, distributed missing regions that require coherent global scene understanding. This is a critical limitation for applications like image editing, data recovery, and robust perception where occlusions are complex. Scaling up to this multi-region problem forces models to reason about long-range semantic dependencies and object interactions, moving beyond local texture synthesis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that formulating multi-region inpainting as a structured inverse problem, where the solution must satisfy both local visual plausibility and global scene consistency, will yield more coherent and semantically accurate completions than applying single-hole methods iteratively.
proposals.method: We will develop a diffusion-based model with a novel attention mechanism. The core is a transformer architecture where visible image tokens and a learnable persistent state vector (inspired by 'Continuous 3D Perception Model') maintain global context. During the reverse diffusion process, we will apply a test-time constraint optimization, using the attention maps between visible and missing region tokens to enforce semantic dependencies (e.g., a filled object in one region must be compatible with an object in another). This builds on 'Visual Jenga's' counterfactual reasoning but scales it to N regions simultaneously.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new benchmark and model for the understudied problem of multi-region inpainting. It will demonstrate how to integrate global scene constraints into a generative inpainting process, advancing the field towards more holistic scene understanding and editing. The proposed attention-based constraint mechanism could be a general tool for structured inverse problems in vision.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly treating the joint completion of multiple, potentially interacting holes as a single inverse optimization problem within a diffusion framework, rather than a sequence of independent tasks. The integration of a persistent scene state with test-time attention-based constraints to enforce inter-region coherence is a new technical direction.

proposals.title: Learning to Colorize from Unlabeled Video via Temporal Token Consistency
proposals.motivation: Current deep learning-based colorization models rely heavily on large-scale, curated datasets of paired (grayscale, color) images, which are expensive and time-consuming to create. This data dependency limits the application and robustness of these models, especially for specialized domains. Leveraging the vast, naturally available source of unlabeled video sequences presents a promising path to circumvent this bottleneck.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a vision transformer, equipped with a persistent memory state, can learn a robust colorization model by enforcing temporal consistency of color tokens across adjacent frames in unlabeled video, without any explicit color supervision.
proposals.method: We will adapt a transformer architecture with a persistent state mechanism, inspired by recent 3D perception models, to process video sequences. The model will be trained to predict color for the current frame using a masked token modeling objective, where color channels of randomly masked patches are the target. The key constraint is a temporal consistency loss applied to the attention-weighted color token representations between consecutive frames, encouraging stable color propagation. This will be combined with a test-time refinement loop that uses the model's own persistent state to iteratively improve consistency on a single video.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel self-supervised framework for image colorization that drastically reduces the need for labeled data. It will advance understanding of how temporal coherence in video can serve as a powerful supervisory signal for low-level vision tasks. The resulting model is expected to demonstrate improved temporal stability and generalization compared to purely image-based counterparts.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses video for representation learning, applying a persistent-state transformer with a temporal token consistency objective specifically for the task of colorization is novel. The approach uniquely combines test-time training with the model's internal memory for refinement, moving beyond one-shot inference.

proposals.title: Counterfactual Colorization: Testing Visual Token Robustness via Distributional Inversion
proposals.motivation: Deep vision models exhibit brittle performance under domain shift, largely due to an over-reliance on the statistical regularities of training datasets like ImageNet. This limits their applicability to real-world data from unseen distributions. We propose to use colorization as a controlled, self-supervised probe to explicitly test and improve a model's ability to separate semantic content from low-level stylistic distribution.
proposals.hypothesis: By training a model to colorize grayscale images from domains increasingly divergent from its training distribution, we can force it to develop robust, content-focused visual token representations that are invariant to domain-specific statistics.
proposals.method: We will build on the 'Visual Jenga' counterfactual framework, treating color as a removable 'object'. A Vision Transformer (ViT) backbone, initialized from a model pre-trained on ImageNet, will be fine-tuned for colorization. Crucially, training will employ a curriculum of progressively out-of-distribution (OOD) grayscale inputs (e.g., sketches, synthetic renderings, satellite imagery). We will analyze the evolving attention patterns between image tokens to identify which become more domain-invariant versus those that remain tied to ImageNet statistics, potentially using untrained registers as probes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel diagnostic for domain robustness and a method to induce it via a challenging reconstruction task. It will yield insights into which layers and attention heads in transformer models capture stylistic vs. semantic information, contributing to more interpretable and generalizable vision architectures.
proposals.novelty: Instead of standard domain adaptation, we actively invert the problem by forcing the model to reconstruct a low-level signal (color) on OOD data, using counterfactual manipulation of style to stress-test semantic token representations. This combines Efros's themes of counterfactual reasoning and token analysis in a novel, distribution-inverting training paradigm.

proposals.title: Counterfactual Colorization: Leveraging Object Dependencies for Rare Scene Realism
proposals.motivation: Current data-driven colorization models, while impressive, often fail on rare objects and scenes due to long-tailed data distributions. Simple class-rebalancing techniques are insufficient as they ignore the complex contextual and semantic dependencies between objects that are crucial for plausible colorization. This work addresses the fundamental challenge of generating realistic colors for infrequent visual concepts by moving beyond per-class statistics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model trained to understand and manipulate the dependencies between objects in a scene—specifically through counterfactual reasoning—can generate more semantically and contextually plausible colors for rare objects by leveraging the color distributions of related, more common co-occurring elements.
proposals.method: We will build upon the 'Visual Jenga' framework for counterfactual inpainting to construct a training objective that explicitly probes object dependencies. A vision transformer (ViT) backbone, potentially augmented with persistent state for temporal coherence, will be trained on a dual-task objective: standard image colorization and a novel 'dependency-aware' colorization loss. This loss will be computed by comparing the model's color predictions for a target object under two conditions: the original context and a counterfactual context where a key related object has been removed or altered via inpainting, forcing the model to learn robust, context-sensitive color associations.
proposals.contribution: This research will deliver a novel colorization model with superior performance on rare and out-of-distribution scenes. It will contribute a new benchmark and evaluation protocol for assessing contextual reasoning in colorization tasks. Furthermore, it will provide insights into how object dependencies can be explicitly modeled for generative vision tasks beyond simple data augmentation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly framing the rare-object colorization problem as one of learning object dependencies, rather than just rebalancing data, and in operationalizing this through a counterfactual training objective derived from visual question-answering and inpainting techniques. This moves beyond prior work focused solely on per-pixel or per-class statistics.

proposals.title: Color from Context: A Symbiotic Transformer for Joint Semantic Segmentation and Image Colorization
proposals.motivation: Image colorization and semantic segmentation are fundamental scene understanding tasks, but are typically solved separately. Colorization models often produce semantically implausible colors (e.g., purple grass) due to a lack of high-level scene understanding, while segmentation models could benefit from richer, color-consistent feature representations. A joint approach could address these weaknesses, leading to more semantically coherent image generation and more robust segmentation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that learning semantic segmentation and image colorization jointly within a single transformer-based architecture will create a synergistic effect: segmentation maps will provide strong semantic priors to guide realistic color prediction, while the colorization task's need for local and global consistency will force the model to learn richer, more discriminative visual features, thereby improving segmentation accuracy.
proposals.method: We will design a dual-branch Vision Transformer (ViT) encoder with a shared backbone and task-specific attention heads, inspired by recent work on efficient multi-task architectures. The model will process grayscale input images. A segmentation decoder will produce semantic masks, while a colorization decoder, conditioned on these masks via cross-attention, will predict per-pixel color distributions in the CIELAB space. We will employ a novel 'Semantic Color Consistency' loss that penalizes color predictions inconsistent with the predicted semantic class (e.g., using learned per-class color histograms from the training data). Training will be end-to-end on datasets like COCO-Stuff or ADE20K.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel multi-task framework demonstrating the symbiosis between low-level color prediction and high-level semantic parsing, 2) a new state-of-the-art in semantic-aware image colorization, and 3) insights into how auxiliary reconstruction tasks can act as powerful self-supervision signals for improving segmentation models without additional labeled data.
proposals.novelty: While multi-task learning is common, the specific pairing of colorization and segmentation is under-explored. The novelty lies in the explicit, bidirectional conditioning within a transformer architecture—using segmentation to constrain colorization via a semantic loss, and using the colorization task's reconstruction objective to regularize and enrich the shared visual features for segmentation.

proposals.title: Unpaired Scene Token Translation via Counterfactual Attention Guidance
proposals.motivation: Image-to-image translation (e.g., style transfer, season change) typically requires paired, aligned training data, which is expensive or impossible to collect for many real-world tasks. While CycleGAN-style methods enable unpaired translation, they often struggle with complex, structured scene transformations, leading to content distortion or mode collapse. A method that reliably translates high-level scene semantics without paired supervision would greatly expand the applicability of image translation to domains like autonomous driving simulation and artistic creation.
proposals.hypothesis: By leveraging a pre-trained vision transformer's internal token representations and a novel counterfactual attention mechanism, we can enforce semantic consistency across unpaired image domains, enabling high-fidelity translation without aligned data.
proposals.method: We will build upon a Vision Transformer (ViT) backbone, treating its patch tokens as a persistent scene state. Inspired by 'Visual Jenga', we will develop a 'Counterfactual Attention Guidance' module that identifies object dependency graphs within a source image. During translation, a cross-domain attention mechanism, operating on these token graphs, will be trained via adversarial and cycle-consistency losses to map source-domain token relationships to plausible target-domain configurations, while a reconstruction loss on 'register' tokens (as per 'Vision Transformers Don't Need Trained Registers') will preserve global scene structure.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new framework for unpaired, structure-aware image translation that explicitly reasons about object dependencies. It will advance the field by providing a more robust and interpretable alternative to purely pixel-level cycle-consistency methods. We will release code and benchmarks for complex scene translation tasks.
proposals.novelty: The approach uniquely combines counterfactual scene graph reasoning from 'Visual Jenga' with the token-based persistent state of a ViT to guide translation, moving beyond pixel/patch alignment to semantic token relationship alignment. This integration for unpaired translation is unexplored.

proposals.title: Meta-Translation: Fast Adaptation of Vision Transformers for Unseen Image-to-Image Tasks
proposals.motivation: Current image translation models, while powerful, require extensive retraining or fine-tuning for each new task (e.g., style transfer, dehazing, sketch-to-photo). This is inefficient and data-hungry, limiting their application to dynamic, user-specified tasks. A model that can rapidly adapt its translation function from only a few examples would be a significant step towards more general and user-friendly visual computing systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a Vision Transformer (ViT) backbone, meta-learned with a model-agnostic meta-learning (MAML) framework, can learn a persistent internal representation of visual concepts that enables rapid adaptation of its cross-attention mechanisms to novel image translation objectives with minimal gradient steps.
proposals.method: We will build upon a pre-trained ViT encoder-decoder architecture designed for dense prediction. The model will be meta-trained on a diverse distribution of image translation tasks (e.g., from datasets like ImageNet-R, AFHQ, and synthetic corruptions). The inner-loop of MAML will adapt a small subset of parameters—specifically the projection layers in cross-attention modules that govern the interaction between source and target image tokens—using only a few paired examples from a held-out task. The outer loop will update the model's initial parameters to optimize for fast adaptation performance across tasks. We will test adaptation speed and quality against standard fine-tuning baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a practical framework for few-shot image translation, reducing the need for large task-specific datasets. It will also provide insights into how meta-learning can instill task-agnostic visual priors within transformer architectures, advancing our understanding of adaptable visual representations.
proposals.novelty: While meta-learning has been explored for classification, its application to conditional image generation and translation with Vision Transformers is less mature. The novelty lies in strategically meta-learning only the cross-modal attention pathways, leaving the bulk of visual features frozen, which aligns with findings that ViTs have robust, persistent representations but need task-specific 'steering'.

proposals.title: Inverting the Generative Process: Recovering Latent Conditions from Diffusion-Model Outputs
proposals.motivation: Modern generative models like diffusion models produce high-quality images from noise, but the mapping from a generated image back to its latent condition (e.g., noise, text embedding) is ill-posed and poorly understood. Solving this inverse problem is crucial for image editing, semantic analysis of generated content, and diagnosing model behavior. It also connects to fundamental questions about the information retained in a model's output.
proposals.hypothesis: A transformer-based network, trained via a contrastive objective on the paired outputs of a frozen diffusion model, can learn to infer the likely latent conditions (noise, text tokens) that generated a given image, even for unseen prompts.
proposals.method: We will train an encoder-decoder transformer to predict the initial noise map and the cross-attention text token embeddings from a generated image. The training dataset will be created by sampling a large set of text prompts, generating images using a frozen Stable Diffusion model, and recording the intermediate latents and attention maps. The model will use a ViT-based image encoder and a causal transformer decoder that attends to both image tokens and a learned persistent memory state (inspired by 'Continuous 3D Perception Model'), enabling it to reason about the generative trajectory. A counterfactual inpainting loss ('Visual Jenga') will be used to enforce that predicted conditions, when fed back into the generator, reconstruct the original image.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical tool for 'inverting' diffusion models, enabling efficient image editing by manipulating recovered latents. It will offer new insights into the information-theoretic relationship between a model's inputs and its outputs. The method could also serve as a novel analysis framework for understanding and visualizing what generative models have learned.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focuses on GAN inversion or encoder-based diffusion, our approach directly learns to map from output image pixels to the full set of initial generative conditions (noise and text) of a standard, frozen diffusion model, framing it as a structured prediction task. The use of a persistent state and counterfactual training specifically targets the recovery of causally relevant latent factors.

proposals.title: Unsupervised Visual Translation via Cyclic Consistency and Duality
proposals.motivation: Unsupervised image-to-image translation, such as CycleGAN, relies on cycle-consistency but often struggles with under-constrained mappings, leading to mode collapse or trivial solutions. Exploiting the inherent duality between paired, inverse visual tasks (e.g., colorization/grayscale, segmentation/rendering) provides a stronger structural constraint. This is particularly relevant for learning robust, semantically meaningful representations without paired data, a core challenge in computer vision.
proposals.hypothesis: By jointly modeling a pair of inverse translation tasks within a single, dual-constrained framework, we can enforce a stricter consistency than standard cycles, leading to more stable training and higher-fidelity, semantically coherent translations in an unsupervised setting.
proposals.method: We propose a dual-consistency framework with two generator networks (G_AB, G_BA) and corresponding discriminators. The core innovation is a dual-consistency loss that enforces not only cycle-consistency (A -> B -> A) but also task-duality consistency: the output of one task (e.g., a colorized image from G_AB) must be a valid input for its inverse task (G_BA), and applying both in sequence should reconstruct the original input through both possible paths. We will implement this using Vision Transformer (ViT) based generators, leveraging their attention mechanisms to model long-range dependencies between image tokens crucial for semantic coherence. Training will use adversarial losses on each domain alongside the novel dual-consistency loss.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a new training paradigm for unsupervised translation that leverages task duality as a supervisory signal. We expect to demonstrate improved quantitative metrics (FID, KID) and qualitative results on established benchmarks (e.g., maps↔aerial photos) compared to CycleGAN and its variants. The framework will provide insights into how structural priors from inverse tasks can regularize generative models.
proposals.novelty: While cycle-consistency is well-established, explicitly formulating and enforcing consistency between the parameters and outputs of two *inverse* translation functions within one model is novel. This moves beyond treating the cycle as a black-box reconstruction to embedding a known functional relationship directly into the learning objective.

proposals.title: Preserving Relational Structure: Cross-Modal Consistency for Source-Free Multi-Task Adaptation
proposals.motivation: Large pre-trained multi-modal and multi-task models (e.g., CLIP, unified vision transformers) are widely deployed, but adapting them to new domains without access to the original source data is a critical, unsolved challenge. Standard fine-tuning on limited target data risks catastrophic forgetting of crucial cross-modal alignments and task interdependencies learned during pre-training. This problem is acute in privacy-sensitive applications (e.g., medical imaging) where source data cannot be retained, necessitating source-free adaptation methods that preserve the model's relational knowledge.
proposals.hypothesis: The structural relationships between modalities and tasks in a pre-trained model provide a rich self-supervisory signal that can be used to constrain and guide adaptation, preventing degradation of these relationships even when source data is unavailable.
proposals.method: We propose a method that treats the pre-trained model's internal relational structure as a 'blueprint' to be preserved. For a vision-language model, we enforce consistency between the visual and textual token embeddings of unlabeled target images via a cross-modal attention distillation loss, using the original frozen model as a teacher. For multi-task models, we adapt the 'Visual Jenga' counterfactual reasoning approach to measure and regularize task dependencies. Adaptation will be performed using a lightweight adapter module (e.g., LoRA) on the transformer's attention layers, minimizing deviation from the original relational attention maps while fitting the target data.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a principled framework for source-free adaptation of complex multi-modal/multi-task models, ensuring robustness in data-constrained scenarios. It will provide new insights into the role of cross-modal and cross-task attention as a carrier of relational knowledge. The resulting method will enable practical deployment of large foundation models while respecting data privacy constraints.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior source-free domain adaptation focused on single modalities, we explicitly regularize the *relationships between modalities or tasks*, not just per-modality features. This moves beyond simple feature alignment to preserving the higher-order structural knowledge that makes these models powerful.

proposals.title: Pixel-Anchored Adaptation: Stabilizing Feature Alignment with a Reversible Consistency Loss
proposals.motivation: Unsupervised domain adaptation (UDA) via feature-level alignment (e.g., with adversarial discriminators) is elegant but can fail silently by distorting semantic features to achieve statistical alignment, harming downstream task performance. This latent instability limits the reliability of UDA in real-world applications where source and target domains differ significantly. A robust, simple regularizer is needed to ensure alignment preserves semantic content.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that enforcing a pixel-space consistency constraint—requiring that adapted features can be decoded back into source-like images—will stabilize feature alignment by preventing the loss of essential visual semantics, leading to more robust adaptation performance.
proposals.method: We build on a standard adversarial feature alignment framework (e.g., using a Gradient Reversal Layer). The core novelty is a lightweight auxiliary decoder network, trained only on source data, which maps adapted features back to pixel space. A consistency loss (L1 or perceptual loss) between the decoder's output and the original source image is added to the adversarial objective. This ensures the feature adapter cannot discard content needed for reconstruction. We will test this on standard UDA benchmarks (Office-31, VisDA) using a Vision Transformer backbone, analyzing failure modes via counterfactual inpaintings.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a simple, plug-in regularizer for feature-based UDA that improves robustness without complex pixel-level cycles. It will offer new insights into the failure modes of adversarial alignment by using pixel-space consistency as a diagnostic tool. The method aims to bridge the conceptual gap between feature-level and pixel-level adaptation strategies.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that uses full image-to-image translation cycles (which are complex and can alter semantics), our method uses a source-only decoder as a lightweight 'anchor' to the source manifold. This provides a direct semantic preservation signal that is orthogonal and complementary to the adversarial alignment objective.

proposals.title: Moment Matching for Adaptation: A Thermodynamic Analogy for Simple Domain Transfer
proposals.motivation: Domain adaptation is crucial for deploying vision models in the real world, where target data distributions (e.g., weather, lighting) differ from the training source. Pre-2026 methods often rely on complex adversarial training or pixel-level style transfer, which can be unstable and computationally expensive. A simpler, more stable principle for distribution alignment is needed to enable robust and efficient adaptation.
proposals.hypothesis: Aligning only the first two statistical moments (mean and covariance) of high-level feature distributions between source and target domains provides a sufficient and stable constraint for effective domain adaptation, analogous to how matching moments of particle distributions predicts macroscopic system properties in thermodynamics.
proposals.method: We will adapt a pre-trained Vision Transformer (ViT) by selecting a critical, high-level layer (e.g., the output of the penultimate transformer block). During adaptation on unlabeled target data, we will compute a batch-wise moment matching loss. This loss will be the sum of the L2 distance between the source and target feature means and the Frobenius norm between their covariance matrices. This loss will be added as a regularization term to the standard cross-entropy loss on the source data, and the network will be fine-tuned end-to-end. We will test this on standard benchmarks like Office-31 and VisDA.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a simple, interpretable, and stable alternative to adversarial domain adaptation methods. It will provide empirical evidence that high-level statistical moment matching is a powerful inductive bias for domain shift, potentially leading to more efficient adaptation protocols. The thermodynamic analogy offers a novel conceptual framework for understanding feature distribution alignment.
proposals.novelty: While moment matching is used in style transfer and batch normalization, applying it as the sole domain adaptation mechanism at a high feature level, inspired by a thermodynamic analogy, is novel. It contrasts with the prevailing pre-2026 trend of multi-layer adversarial alignment or complex pixel-space transformations.

proposals.title: Spectral CycleGANs: Unsupervised Audio-Text Domain Adaptation via Frequency and Attention Distribution Alignment
proposals.motivation: Unsupervised domain adaptation (UDA) is well-studied for vision, but methods for sequential, non-visual data like audio and text remain less explored. Models trained on clean, labeled audio or formal text often fail on noisy recordings or informal genres, creating a deployment bottleneck. Drawing an analogy from geophysics, where spectral alignment adapts seismic signals across surveys, offers a principled path to bridge these distribution gaps without paired data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a cycle-consistent adversarial framework, applied not to raw signals but to their core spectral or latent distributions, can learn bidirectional mappings that preserve content while aligning domains for audio and text, enabling effective UDA.
proposals.method: We will implement a dual-stream CycleGAN architecture. For audio, generators will transform mel-spectrograms between domains (e.g., clean↔noisy), with discriminators operating on the spectral magnitude distributions. For text, we will use a pretrained transformer (e.g., BERT) to extract token embeddings and self-attention maps; generators will be lightweight adaptor networks that transform these latent distributions, and discriminators will classify the domain of the attention/embedding statistics. Cycle-consistency and identity losses will enforce content preservation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, general framework for UDA on sequential, non-visual data, validated on tasks like noisy speech recognition and cross-genre text classification. It will demonstrate the utility of cross-disciplinary analogies from signal processing for ML and yield insights into what constitutes a 'domain' in audio and text feature spaces.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the direct adaptation of spectral alignment principles from seismic processing to ML and the specific focus on aligning transformer attention distributions for text, rather than simpler feature vectors, which is unexplored in pre-2026 UDA literature.

proposals.title: Contrastive State Propagation: Learning Domain-Invariant 3D Scene Representations via Counterfactual Augmentation
proposals.motivation: Current 3D perception models, including those with persistent state, often fail to generalize across domains due to spurious correlations in visual data. This limits their robustness in real-world applications like autonomous systems. Pre-2026 contrastive learning has shown promise for 2D domain invariance, but its integration into continuous, stateful 3D scene understanding remains underexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying contrastive losses to the persistent state tokens of a 3D perception model, using counterfactual image augmentations inspired by visual 'Jenga', will force the model to learn object-centric, domain-invariant 3D features that generalize better across visual domains.
proposals.method: We will extend a transformer-based continuous 3D perception model with a persistent memory bank. During training, we will create positive pairs by applying domain-preserving geometric augmentations (e.g., viewpoint shifts) to the same underlying 3D scene state, and negative pairs using counterfactual inpainting to alter object dependencies. A contrastive InfoNCE loss will be applied directly to the model's persistent state tokens, encouraging invariance to nuisance factors while preserving essential 3D structure. This will be integrated with the standard reconstruction loss.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel training framework for building more robust and generalizable 3D world models. It will demonstrate how contrastive learning on structured scene representations, rather than raw pixels, improves cross-domain generalization. The resulting model will be evaluated on challenging domain shift benchmarks for 3D scene understanding.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the targeted application of contrastive learning to the persistent state of a continuous 3D model, using counterfactual scene manipulations as a principled source of negative samples, moving beyond simple image-level augmentations.

proposals.title: Synergistic Synthesis and Discovery: Closing the Loop for Unsupervised Compositional Vision
proposals.motivation: Current unsupervised representation learning methods, like those using vision transformers and attention, can discover object-centric features. However, evaluating and refining these discovered concepts without labels is challenging. We lack a systematic way to test a model's understanding of part-whole relationships and compositional rules, hindering progress towards human-like scene understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: By using an unsupervised feature discovery model to guide a pixel-level image translator, we can synthesize targeted 'feature probe' images that, when fed back into the discovery model, will expose its compositional failures and enable iterative, self-improving learning of object parts and their relations.
proposals.method: We will build a two-module system. First, an unsupervised discovery model (e.g., a slot-attention or transformer-based encoder) will decompose scenes into a set of feature tokens. Second, a conditional image synthesis model (e.g., a diffusion model or GAN) will be trained to translate between images by manipulating these discovered tokens—swapping, adding, or removing them. The synthesis model will generate counterfactual probes (e.g., a car with a missing wheel, a novel animal part combination). The discovery model's consistency and attention patterns on these synthetic samples will be used as a training signal to refine both modules in a closed loop.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for unsupervised evaluation and bootstrapping of compositional vision models. It will yield a diagnostic toolkit of synthetic feature probes and demonstrate a proof-of-concept for label-free, iterative refinement of visual concept learning, moving beyond static dataset training.
proposals.novelty: Unlike using data augmentation for robustness or image translation for style transfer, we repurpose synthesis specifically as a *diagnostic and refinement tool* for the discovery model itself, creating a synergistic feedback loop not present in prior work.

proposals.title: Pixel-Level Translation as a Semantic Data Augmentation Engine
proposals.motivation: Data augmentation is crucial for training robust vision models, but traditional methods like cropping and color jittering operate at a low, pixel-statistic level, failing to alter high-level semantics. Recent pixel-level image-to-image translation models (e.g., based on diffusion or GANs) can generate highly realistic and diverse visual content. This project investigates whether such translators can be repurposed as a powerful, semantically-aware data augmentation tool to improve model generalization and sample efficiency, a key challenge in pre-2026 computer vision.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a pixel-level translator, trained to perform unconditional or weakly-guided synthesis, can generate semantically consistent and diverse variations of training images. Using these synthesized images for augmentation will lead to improved model robustness and generalization compared to standard augmentation techniques, especially in low-data regimes.
proposals.method: We will leverage a pre-trained diffusion-based image-to-image translation model as a persistent state generator. For a given training image, we will use attention-based cross-modal guidance (e.g., from CLIP text embeddings of the image's class or a segmentation mask) to produce multiple, diverse renditions while preserving core object semantics, inspired by 'counterfactual' edits from Visual Jenga. We will systematically test augmentation strategies: interpolating between original and translated images in latent space, and using translated images directly. The augmented datasets will be used to train standard Vision Transformer (ViT) classifiers, evaluating performance on held-out and out-of-distribution test sets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for semantic data augmentation using generative models, moving beyond low-level transformations. It will deliver a systematic analysis of how translation-based augmentation affects model robustness and generalization, with benchmarks against state-of-the-art methods. The findings could establish a new, practical use case for image translation models in the training pipeline.
proposals.novelty: While image translation is well-studied for synthesis and editing, its systematic application as a controlled, semantic data augmentation engine is novel. The approach is distinct from simple GAN-based augmentation as it leverages the persistent state and attention mechanisms of modern translators to ensure semantic fidelity while maximizing diversity, a non-obvious dual-use of these models.

proposals.title: Domain Confusion via Inverted Translation: Learning Invariant Features by Reversing the Translation Gradient
proposals.motivation: Current unsupervised domain adaptation methods often rely on adversarial feature alignment or explicit translation, which can be unstable or require complex cycle-consistency constraints. A more direct method for learning domain-invariant representations, grounded in the mechanics of image-to-image translation, is needed for robust perception across visual domains like synthetic-to-real or across weather conditions. This is crucial for models that must maintain performance in the wild without access to labeled target data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by inverting the gradient signal in a standard image translation model—training the translator to fail at creating domain-specific artifacts—we can force the shared encoder to learn features that are maximally confusing to the domain discriminator, thus achieving superior domain invariance compared to adversarial alignment.
proposals.method: We will build upon a U-Net or Vision Transformer based image translation framework (e.g., Pix2Pix). The key innovation is to modify the training objective: while the generator G aims to translate image X from domain A to domain B, we will use a gradient reversal layer between the shared feature encoder E and the domain classifier D. Instead of D learning to classify the domain, we will train E to maximize D's confusion on the source of features (A vs. B), while G is simultaneously trained with a reconstruction loss on its *inability* to alter domain-specific style, preserving content. Attention mechanisms within the transformer blocks will be analyzed to identify which tokens become domain-agnostic.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, stable training paradigm for domain-invariant feature learning that directly leverages and inverts translation architectures. We will provide a new diagnostic, 'translation invertibility,' to measure invariance, and release a benchmark based on counterfactual inpaintings from 'Visual Jenga' to test robustness to object dependencies across domains.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct repurposing of a translation network's objective to induce failure, creating a form of 'built-in' domain confusion, rather than adding a separate adversarial network. It connects the concrete process of pixel translation to abstract feature invariance in a uniquely invertible way.

proposals.title: Direct Feature Jenga: Unsupervised Image Translation via Counterfactual Embedding Manipulation
proposals.motivation: Image-to-image translation typically relies on complex generator networks, which are computationally expensive to train and prone to mode collapse. Recent work in contrastive learning (e.g., CLIP) has shown that semantically rich, aligned embedding spaces can be learned from raw image-text pairs. This proposal asks if we can bypass the generator entirely for many translation tasks by directly manipulating features within such a pre-trained, frozen embedding space.
proposals.hypothesis: Semantic image translation can be achieved by identifying and swapping discrete, causally relevant 'object tokens' in a contrastive image embedding, followed by a direct mapping from the manipulated embedding back to pixel space using a lightweight, non-adversarial decoder.
proposals.method: We will build on the 'Visual Jenga' counterfactual framework to identify object-dependent feature tokens in a pre-trained vision transformer (ViT) encoder. For a given image pair (source, target domain), we will compute attention-based causal masks to isolate object-specific token sets. Translation will be performed by swapping these token sets between images in the frozen contrastive embedding space. A small, time-conditioned diffusion decoder (inspired by recent test-time training methods) will be trained to invert these manipulated embeddings directly to pixels, eliminating the need for a paired GAN generator.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate a new paradigm for image translation that is more efficient and interpretable than generator-based methods. It will provide a formal link between causal scene understanding, contrastive representation learning, and image synthesis, potentially leading to more controllable and data-efficient vision models.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses embeddings for guidance or loss functions, we propose a fully generator-free translation pipeline where the core semantic operation is a discrete token swap in a frozen model. This combines counterfactual reasoning from Visual Jenga with the robust representations of contrastive models in a novel, non-adversarial synthesis framework.

proposals.title: Contrastive Scene Tokens: Unifying Instance Discrimination and Generative Modeling for Visual Representation Learning
proposals.motivation: Self-supervised learning has advanced through distinct paradigms: contrastive methods (e.g., SimCLR, MoCo) excel at instance discrimination, while generative methods (e.g., masked autoencoders, BEiT) learn rich spatial context. However, these objectives are typically pursued in isolation, potentially learning complementary scene properties. A unified framework could yield more robust and semantically grounded visual representations, capturing both the invariance of objects and the compositional structure of scenes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that jointly training a Vision Transformer with a contrastive loss on global image tokens and a masked token modeling loss on local patch tokens will produce visual features that outperform those from either objective alone, particularly on tasks requiring understanding of object relationships and scene structure.
proposals.method: We will train a Vision Transformer backbone with two parallel heads. A contrastive head will apply an InfoNCE loss on a [CLS] token, using standard augmentations. Simultaneously, a generative head will reconstruct masked patch tokens via a cross-attention decoder, as in MAE, but applied to the intermediate transformer features. A key design is to use the persistent state concept from Efros's prior work to maintain a consistent scene representation between the two objectives. We will experiment with loss weighting schedules and test the impact of sharing attention mechanisms across tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled framework for multi-objective self-supervised learning in vision. We expect to demonstrate state-of-the-art transfer performance on downstream tasks like semantic segmentation and object detection, and to offer new insights into the synergies between discriminative and generative pre-training. The released model will serve as a strong baseline for unified representation learning.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has combined objectives like contrastive and clustering, our approach uniquely integrates a global contrastive loss with a fine-grained masked generative loss within a single transformer, leveraging a shared persistent state to align the two learning signals at the scene level.

proposals.title: Global-Local Visual Correspondence: Unifying Patch and Image-Level Contrastive Learning
proposals.motivation: Current self-supervised visual representation learning often operates at a single scale, either via patch-level matching (e.g., DINO, iBOT) or global image-level contrast (e.g., CLIP, SimCLR). This creates a representational gap: patch methods excel at local feature matching but can struggle with holistic scene understanding, while global methods capture semantic similarity but may lose fine-grained spatial correspondence. Bridging this gap is crucial for tasks requiring both semantic and geometric reasoning, such as 3D perception and counterfactual scene editing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that jointly training a Vision Transformer (ViT) with a combined loss that enforces both patch-level feature consistency and a novel global image-level contrastive objective will yield representations that are superior for dense matching and holistic understanding compared to single-scale objectives.
proposals.method: We will train a standard ViT backbone with two parallel heads. The first head will implement a patch-level self-distillation loss, inspired by DINO/iBOT, using cross-entropy between patch token features from differently augmented views of an image. The second head will produce a global image representation via a [CLS] token or a novel aggregation of patch tokens (exploring alternatives to trained registers). A global contrastive loss (e.g., InfoNCE) will be applied between these global representations of different augmented views. The model will be trained end-to-end, balancing the patch and global losses. We will test on established benchmarks for image matching, segmentation transfer, and retrieval.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified framework for learning visual representations that are semantically coherent at the image level and geometrically consistent at the patch level. It will deliver a single model that advances the state-of-the-art on both dense correspondence tasks (e.g., semantic matching) and holistic understanding tasks (e.g., image classification, retrieval). The analysis will offer new insights into the interplay between local and global features in self-supervised learning.
proposals.novelty: While multi-task learning is common, the specific co-training of a patch-level *correspondence* loss (not just classification) with a global *contrastive* loss is unexplored. The novelty lies in explicitly designing the global objective to be complementary to, not redundant with, the patch-level objective, forcing the model to integrate information across scales.

proposals.title: Cross-Domain Retrieval via Translation-Equivariant Contrastive Features
proposals.motivation: Image retrieval systems excel within domains but struggle across domains (e.g., sketch-to-photo, synthetic-to-real). Pre-2026 vision-language models like CLIP provide a joint embedding space, but their features are often optimized for global semantic alignment, not for preserving structural correspondences crucial for fine-grained retrieval. A method that leverages the latent structure of image-to-image translation models could bridge this gap.
proposals.hypothesis: The intermediate feature space of a contrastively trained image-to-image translation model inherently encodes translation-equivariant representations, making it superior for cross-domain retrieval tasks compared to standard contrastive vision-language embeddings.
proposals.method: We will train a conditional image translation model (e.g., based on a diffusion model or a GAN) using a contrastive loss (like InfoNCE) on paired cross-domain data (e.g., sketches and photos). During training, we will enforce consistency between the feature tokens of the source image and corresponding patches in the generated target image via a cross-attention mechanism. For retrieval, we will extract multi-scale feature tokens from the translation model's encoder and use their spatial similarity, aggregated via attention pooling, to compute a robust cross-domain similarity score.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a new paradigm for cross-domain retrieval that leverages the internal representations of translation models. It will provide a benchmark and analysis showing that translation-equivariant features outperform standard CLIP-style embeddings on fine-grained, structure-sensitive retrieval tasks across diverse domains.
proposals.novelty: Instead of using an embedding space trained for discrimination or generation alone, we explicitly exploit the *translation task* to learn an embedding space where structural consistency, not just semantic similarity, is the primary organizing principle for retrieval.

proposals.title: One-Shot Scene Translation via Persistent State and Counterfactual Attention
proposals.motivation: Current image-to-image translation models require extensive datasets per target domain, limiting their application to novel or rare visual styles captured in only a handful of examples. This problem is critical for personalization, historical media restoration, and adapting vision systems to niche environments. A method that can learn a translation from just one or a few exemplars would dramatically increase the utility and accessibility of these models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by leveraging a persistent, scene-level state model and a counterfactual reasoning mechanism, a vision transformer can disentangle and recombine object-level and stylistic attributes from a single example, enabling robust one-shot translation.
proposals.method: We will build upon a pre-trained vision transformer (ViT) backbone, integrating a learned persistent state vector to maintain a consistent scene representation across the translation process, inspired by recent 3D perception models. The core adaptation will use a novel 'counterfactual attention' module, extending ideas from Visual Jenga, to identify and manipulate dependencies between image tokens representing objects and their stylistic attributes. Training will employ a meta-learning setup on a large corpus of diverse images, where at test time the model must adapt to a new target domain using only one or a few reference images, freezing the backbone and fine-tuning only lightweight adapters.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical framework for extreme few-shot image translation, validated on benchmarks like AFHQ and MetFaces. It will contribute a new understanding of how to use attention mechanisms for attribute disentanglement in a data-starved regime and release a model that significantly lowers the data barrier for style transfer applications.
proposals.novelty: The combination of a persistent state model for scene coherence with counterfactual attention for one-shot attribute manipulation is unexplored. Unlike prior few-shot translation works that rely on extensive latent alignment or optimization, our approach explicitly reasons about object-style dependencies within the transformer's token space.

proposals.title: Inverting the Knowledge Flow: Training Generative Models via Learned Editing Priors
proposals.motivation: Current generative models (e.g., diffusion models) are trained on massive, often noisy datasets, learning a data distribution that may encode unwanted biases or lack fine-grained control. Conversely, recent image editing models, like those for counterfactual inpainting, learn powerful, structured priors about object relationships and scene semantics. This project asks if we can invert the typical pipeline: using a trained editing model as a source of structured knowledge to guide or train a generative model, potentially leading to more data-efficient, controllable, and semantically coherent generation.
proposals.hypothesis: A high-performance image editing model, trained to perform semantically meaningful manipulations (e.g., object removal, insertion, rearrangement), encodes a stronger and more useful prior over scene structure than raw pixel statistics. This prior can be distilled to train or regularize a generative model, improving its sample quality and controllability with less data.
proposals.method: We will first train a state-of-the-art editing model, building on architectures like a Vision Transformer with specialized attention mechanisms for inpainting (inspired by 'Visual Jenga'), on a dataset of real images. This model will learn to predict plausible scene completions. We will then use this frozen editor as a 'teacher' in a two-stage process: 1) A generative model (e.g., a latent diffusion model) generates a candidate image. 2) The editor is applied to create a semantically edited variant (e.g., removing a generated object). The difference between the original and edited outputs, according to the editor's own consistency metrics, will form a novel training signal—an 'editing loss'—that is backpropagated to update the generative model, encouraging it to produce images that are easily editable and thus structurally coherent.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a new paradigm for training generative models using learned editing operators as a source of supervision. It will demonstrate a method for injecting high-level scene structure priors into generative pipelines, potentially reducing reliance on vast datasets. The resulting models are expected to show improved compositional generalization and offer new interfaces for control via the editing model's latent space.
proposals.novelty: While knowledge distillation is common, distilling knowledge from a *task-specific editing model* to a *general generative model* inverts the typical data flow. This is distinct from using pre-trained generators for editing or co-training; here, the editor is the primary source of learned knowledge, actively guiding the generator's training from scratch or early stages.

proposals.title: Cycle3D: Unpaired Geometric Translation via Latent Cycle-Consistency
proposals.motivation: While unpaired image-to-image translation via cycle-consistency (e.g., CycleGAN) is mature, its application to 3D geometric data remains underexplored. A robust method for translating between 3D shape domains (e.g., chairs to tables, synthetic to real-scanned meshes) without paired supervision would unlock large, unstructured 3D datasets for tasks like content creation, domain adaptation for robotics, and data augmentation. This addresses a key gap in 3D vision, where paired, aligned data is exceptionally costly to obtain.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a transformer-based architecture, trained with a cycle-consistency loss on a persistent 3D latent state, can learn semantically meaningful and geometrically valid translations between unpaired collections of 3D shapes or scene representations.
proposals.method: We will represent 3D shapes as sequences of geometric tokens (e.g., from a pre-trained 3D tokenizer like ShapeGPT or using point cloud patches). A transformer encoder-decoder model will maintain a persistent latent state for a shape, inspired by 'Continuous 3D Perception Model' work. The core training will use adversarial losses for domain translation, coupled with a cycle-consistency loss applied in this latent space to enforce semantic preservation. We will test time efficiency by comparing against autoencoder baselines and evaluate on tasks like unpaired shape completion (inspired by 'Visual Jenga') and synthetic-to-real geometric translation.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel framework for unpaired 3D geometric translation, extending the cycle-consistency principle into the 3D domain. It will provide benchmarks and analysis for the fidelity and semantic consistency of such translations, offering a new tool for 3D content generation and domain adaptation without requiring costly paired data.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying the cycle-consistency framework—prevalent in 2D—to the structured, continuous domain of 3D geometry using a transformer with persistent state, moving beyond style transfer to fundamental geometric and structural translation. This is distinct from prior 3D GANs focused on single-domain generation.

proposals.title: Meta-Translation: Few-Shot Domain Adaptation for Image-to-Image Translation via Attention-Based Meta-Learning
proposals.motivation: Image-to-image translation models excel in specific domains but require extensive retraining for new ones, limiting their practical utility. Few-shot adaptation is crucial for applications like artistic style transfer or domain-specific medical imaging where target data is scarce. Current methods often rely on fine-tuning, which can overfit to the few examples and degrade performance on the original domain.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a meta-learning framework, which trains a translation model's attention mechanisms to be rapidly reconfigurable, can enable effective few-shot adaptation to new visual domains while preserving performance on the source domain.
proposals.method: We will build upon a Vision Transformer (ViT)-based translation architecture, treating its cross-attention layers as the primary adaptation mechanism. Using a Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) framework, we will train the model on a distribution of translation tasks (e.g., various style transfers). The inner loop will adapt only the parameters of specific attention layers using a few target examples, while the outer loop will update the model's initialization to be optimal for this fast adaptation. We will test this against standard fine-tuning baselines and analyze the role of persistent state tokens in stabilizing the adaptation process.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical framework for data-efficient image translation, reducing the need for large target datasets. It will provide new insights into which components of a transformer-based translation model are most critical for fast adaptation, linking meta-learning with visual token dynamics.
proposals.novelty: While meta-learning has been explored for classification, its application to conditional generative tasks like image translation, specifically by targeting the reconfigurability of attention mechanisms rather than all parameters, is novel. This approach directly connects to Efros's recent work on persistent state and efficient transformers.

proposals.title: Perceptual Priors from Abstract Domains: Challenging the Optimality of ImageNet Features
proposals.motivation: The field of computer vision has long assumed that features learned from large-scale, natural image datasets like ImageNet are the optimal foundation for perceptual tasks. This assumption, however, conflates statistical sufficiency with perceptual necessity. Recent work in multimodal and self-supervised learning suggests that semantic structure can emerge from diverse data modalities, prompting a fundamental question: are visual features uniquely privileged for perception, or can abstract, non-visual representations capture similar perceptual invariances?
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that deep networks trained on structured, non-visual data (e.g., audio spectrograms, symbolic music, or protein sequences) can learn features that are transferable to core visual perception tasks, performing on par with or complementing ImageNet-pretrained features in certain domains.
proposals.method: We will train identical Vision Transformer (ViT) architectures on several curated, non-visual datasets with rich structural tokens: AudioSet (spectrograms), symbolic MIDI sequences, and protein fold databases. Using a 'Visual Jenga'-inspired counterfactual framework, we will systematically test the perceptual quality of these features by probing them on established benchmarks for image similarity, texture discrimination, and few-shot object recognition. A key analysis will involve cross-modal attention maps between abstract tokens and image patches to discover shared structural priors, extending methods from 'Vision Transformers Don't Need Trained Registers' for model introspection.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic challenge to the presumed optimality of ImageNet features, potentially uncovering domain-agnostic structural priors useful for perception. It will contribute a new experimental framework for evaluating 'perceptualness' divorced from visual data and could inform more efficient, multimodal foundation model training.
proposals.novelty: While prior work studies transfer from vision to non-visual domains, we invert this pipeline to test if perception can *emerge from* abstract domains. This directly tests a deep-seated assumption in the field using Efros group's signature methods for causal analysis of visual representations.

proposals.title: Shallow Tokens, Deep Semantics: Recovering Hierarchical Features with Counterfactual Supervision
proposals.motivation: Deep convolutional and transformer architectures dominate visual representation learning, but their depth and complexity raise questions about necessity versus sufficiency. Given the success of Vision Transformers (ViTs) and the analysis in works like 'Vision Transformers Don't Need Trained Registers', we must ask if the hierarchical features they learn are an emergent property of depth or a byproduct of the training objective and data. This work challenges the architectural dogma by exploring if shallow models can directly learn semantically rich, compositional features typically associated with deep layers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that with a training paradigm focused on exposing object dependencies and scene structure—inspired by counterfactual reasoning—a very shallow network or a non-neural model operating on image tokens can learn feature representations that are functionally equivalent to those from deep middle layers of standard models.
proposals.method: We will design a shallow Vision Transformer (e.g., 2-4 layers) or a lightweight non-neural graph model over superpixel/patch tokens.
