The core innovation is the training objective: we will employ a 'Visual Jenga'-inspired counterfactual inpainting task, where the model must predict the properties of a missing image region conditioned on the persistent state of the rest of the scene. This forces the model to internalize object relationships and part-whole hierarchies without deep sequential processing. We will compare the learned token embeddings from our shallow model to the intermediate layer features of a standard, pre-trained deep ViT using Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA) and their performance on linear probing for downstream tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous test for the necessity of depth in visual representation learning. It could lead to more efficient, interpretable models for scene understanding. If successful, it would decouple feature hierarchy from architectural depth, suggesting that training tasks, not just model capacity, are primary drivers of semantic representation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has studied model compression or efficient architectures, our approach uniquely inverts the problem: we aim to match deep *internal representations* of a frozen teacher using a radically shallow student, enabled by a novel counterfactual training objective that explicitly teaches scene compositionality.

proposals.title: The Emergence of Perceptual Metrics: A Causal and Invariance-Based Theory for Deep Visual Features
proposals.motivation: A long-standing observation in deep learning is that features from networks trained on image classification exhibit perceptual similarity, aligning with human judgments better than pixel-level metrics. However, a rigorous theoretical explanation for this emergent property is lacking. Understanding this phenomenon is crucial for building more robust, interpretable, and human-aligned vision models, especially as they are increasingly used in generative tasks and content creation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the perceptual quality of deep features emerges from the causal structure of natural images and the invariance objectives of standard training. Specifically, we posit that networks learn to discard nuisance factors (e.g., exact pixel arrangement) while preserving the causal, object-level factors that are predictive across diverse tasks, thereby aligning their internal representations with perceptually relevant image properties.
proposals.method: We will design a series of controlled experiments using Vision Transformers (ViTs) and CNNs. Inspired by 'Visual Jenga', we will use counterfactual image generation to systematically manipulate nuisance variables (e.g., texture, exact edges) and causal object variables (e.g., part geometry, relational structure). We will measure feature-space distances (using attention maps and token representations) before and after manipulation. We will analyze training dynamics, testing the effect of different training objectives (classification, masked autoencoding) and regularization (e.g., via persistent state models) on the rate at which perceptual alignment emerges in the feature hierarchy.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, testable framework linking the perceptual properties of deep features to the causal and invariance-based structure of their training. It will yield new diagnostic tools for evaluating representation quality and may inform the design of novel loss functions or architectures that explicitly optimize for perceptual alignment, bridging the gap between empirical observation and theory.
proposals.novelty: While the perceptual effect is widely noted, our approach is novel in framing it as a direct consequence of learning causal invariances, moving beyond correlation to a mechanistic explanation. We uniquely combine counterfactual analysis from 'Visual Jenga' with the analysis of training dynamics and architectural choices (like attention in ViTs) to isolate the contributing factors.

proposals.title: Temporal Perceptual Similarity: A Deep Feature Metric for Video Quality and Synthesis
proposals.motivation: Perceptual metrics like LPIPS and DISTS, built on deep feature similarity, have become the gold standard for evaluating image synthesis and quality. However, video evaluation still relies heavily on frame-wise averages of these image metrics, failing to account for critical temporal factors like motion realism, temporal consistency, and flicker. As video generation and compression advance, a dedicated perceptual video metric is urgently needed to guide progress.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a video perceptual metric can be constructed by extending deep feature similarity frameworks to incorporate spatiotemporal features, and that such a metric will correlate better with human judgments of video quality than frame-averaged image metrics.
proposals.method: We will leverage pre-trained video understanding models (e.g., inflated 3D CNNs or Video Swin Transformers) as feature extractors. The core metric will compute feature distance between two video clips, but we will explicitly model temporal relationships. Inspired by recent work on persistent state and attention, we will design a spatiotemporal pooling mechanism that aggregates features across time, perhaps using a learned temporal attention module to weight perceptually critical moments. The final distance function will be calibrated on human video quality judgment datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first dedicated, learned perceptual similarity metric for video, providing a crucial tool for evaluating video generation, compression, and restoration algorithms. It will also offer insights into which spatiotemporal features are most salient for human perception of video quality.
proposals.novelty: While image metrics are mature, constructing a video metric is not a simple temporal extension; it requires novel architectural choices for spatiotemporal feature integration and pooling, moving beyond naive frame averaging to capture perceptual phenomena like motion coherence.

proposals.title: PerceptualBench: Scaling Human Judgments for Robust Visual Representation Learning
proposals.motivation: Current perceptual similarity benchmarks (e.g., BAPPS, PieAPP) are limited in scale and diversity, failing to capture the full complexity of human visual judgment across varied scenes, textures, and semantic distortions. This bottleneck hinders the training and evaluation of vision models, particularly vision transformers and generative models, which rely on accurate perceptual metrics for tasks like image synthesis, editing, and 3D scene understanding. A more comprehensive dataset is needed to move beyond narrow, artifact-focused evaluations.
proposals.hypothesis: A large-scale, diverse dataset of human perceptual judgments will reveal systematic biases in current perceptual metrics (e.g., LPIPS, DISTS) and enable the training of a more robust, generalizable similarity model that better aligns with human vision across a wider distribution of image transformations.
proposals.method: We will construct a dataset orders of magnitude larger than existing ones by leveraging a scalable, game-like crowdsourcing framework inspired by 'Visual Jenga', presenting triplets of images (reference, distortion A, distortion B) for relative similarity judgment. The image corpus will be strategically diversified using generative models (e.g., Stable Diffusion) and counterfactual inpainting to create controlled distortions across semantic, geometric, and textural dimensions. We will then train a vision transformer-based metric, incorporating a persistent memory mechanism (inspired by 'Continuous 3D Perception') to model contextual dependencies, and use attention layers to weight perceptual features dynamically, moving beyond simple weighted L2 distances in a fixed feature space.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) A large-scale, public benchmark for perceptual similarity, filling a critical data gap. 2) A new state-of-the-art perceptual metric that generalizes better to novel image types and distortions. 3) Insights into the failure modes of current metrics, guiding future model design for tasks requiring human-aligned visual evaluation.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior datasets focused on synthetic distortions or a narrow image set, our benchmark explicitly targets semantic and structural diversity generated via modern synthesis tools. Furthermore, the proposed model leverages persistent state and attention mechanisms—concepts from Efros's recent work—to capture the non-local and contextual nature of human similarity judgments, rather than treating them as pointwise comparisons.

proposals.title: Perceptual Metric Supervision: Training Vision Transformers with Human Similarity Judgments
proposals.motivation: Deep visual features are typically trained for tasks like classification or reconstruction, which may not align with human perceptual judgments of image similarity. This misalignment can limit the utility of these features for applications requiring human-like scene understanding, such as image retrieval, editing, or generation. Leveraging pre-2026 datasets of human perceptual similarity (e.g., BAM, THINGS) offers a direct, task-agnostic signal to bridge this gap.
proposals.hypothesis: Supervising a Vision Transformer's feature space with a loss derived from human perceptual similarity judgments will yield features that are more semantically and perceptually aligned with human vision than those trained solely on standard proxy tasks like ImageNet classification.
proposals.method: We will train a Vision Transformer (ViT) backbone where, in addition to a standard self-supervised objective (e.g., masked autoencoding), we introduce a perceptual metric loss. This loss will be computed between the [CLS] tokens of image pairs, using a contrastive objective (e.g., InfoNCE) where positive pairs are defined by high human-rated similarity from a pre-existing dataset. The model will be trained to pull the features of perceptually similar images together in the latent space, while pushing apart dissimilar ones, directly shaping the feature manifold.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate a method for directly injecting human perceptual priors into deep feature learning. We expect the resulting features to show improved performance on downstream tasks requiring semantic and perceptual alignment, such as zero-shot retrieval and image analogy tasks, and to provide a more interpretable feature space that better reflects human judgments.
proposals.novelty: While metric learning is well-established, directly using large-scale, task-agnostic human perceptual judgments as the sole supervisory signal for deep feature learning is unexplored. This approach moves beyond using human data for evaluation and makes it a core, driving component of the training objective for a general-purpose visual encoder.

proposals.title: Cross-Image Spatial Context Modeling via Visual Coreference Resolution
proposals.motivation: Current vision models, including Vision Transformers, primarily reason about spatial relationships within a single image, limiting their ability to build a coherent, persistent understanding of the visual world from multiple observations. This mirrors a pre-2026 limitation in NLP where early models struggled with coreference across documents. Enabling models to link visual 'mentions' (patches/objects) across different images and infer their relative spatial context would be a significant step towards more holistic scene understanding and data-efficient learning.
proposals.hypothesis: By adapting the paradigm of cross-document coreference resolution to the visual domain, we can train a model to project patches from semantically related images into a shared latent space and reliably predict geometric relationships (e.g., 'left of', 'contains') between them, thereby learning a generalized, cross-image model of spatial context.
proposals.method: We will train a dual-encoder Transformer model where one encoder processes a 'query' image patch and the other processes a 'context' image. Drawing from methods in 'Visual Jenga' for counterfactual reasoning and persistent state models, we will treat patches as visual tokens. A cross-attention mechanism will be used to resolve coreference, aligning patches that refer to the same semantic concept or object part across images. The model will be trained on curated datasets of related images (e.g., different views of the same scene category) with supervised objectives for coreference linking and relative spatial predicate classification between linked patches.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a new task and benchmark for cross-image spatial reasoning. It will provide a model capable of building persistent spatial representations from disparate visual observations, contributing to more data-efficient and context-aware vision systems. The coreference mechanism offers a novel way to share and propagate information across image boundaries during inference.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is directly translating the cross-document coreference framework, common in NLP, to the problem of visual spatial relationship prediction, moving beyond the standard within-image assumption. This approach is distinct from simple image-pair retrieval or multi-view geometry, as it focuses on learning a generalizable function for predicting spatial relations between semantically linked but visually distinct patches.

proposals.title: Cross-Image Contextual Prediction: Learning Scene Priors Beyond Single Images
proposals.motivation: Current vision models, including Vision Transformers, learn powerful representations by predicting masked patches within a single image. However, this within-image objective fails to capture the fundamental regularities of how objects and scenes are arranged across the visual world. A model that truly understands scene structure should be able to reason about spatial relationships between entities across different instances of semantically similar scenes, a capability crucial for robust scene understanding, generation, and counterfactual reasoning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that training a model to predict the spatial configuration of patches from one image, given a context patch from a different but semantically related image, will force it to learn a more general and disentangled representation of object relationships and scene geometry.
proposals.method: We will build on masked autoencoder (MAE) and Visual Jenga-style counterfactual frameworks. Given a pair of semantically related images (e.g., two different kitchens), we will extract a query patch from one image and a set of candidate patches from the other. The model, a Vision Transformer encoder, must attend to the query and predict the correct spatial position of a masked target region in the second image relative to the query. Training will involve a contrastive loss over spatial coordinate tokens, encouraging the model's attention mechanism to align cross-image contextual cues. We will test on datasets with rich scene structure (e.g., Hypersim, COCO).
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel pre-training objective that explicitly learns cross-image scene priors. It will provide a new benchmark for evaluating models' understanding of scene geometry independent of specific instance appearance. The resulting representations are expected to improve performance on downstream tasks requiring structural reasoning, such as 3D perception from single images and semantic inpainting.
proposals.novelty: While prior work like Visual Jenga explores object dependencies via inpainting within a single image, and MAE learns within-image reconstruction, our method is the first to directly train a model to reason about spatial context *across* images, breaking the fundamental within-image assumption of most self-supervised vision objectives.

proposals.title: JigsawPaint: Learning Unified Visual Representations from Adaptive, Overlapping Context Patches
proposals.motivation: Self-supervised learning for vision has advanced through tasks like jigsaw puzzles, colorization, and inpainting, but these are often studied in isolation or with fixed, non-overlapping input partitions. This fails to capture the continuous, multi-scale nature of visual scenes. A framework that jointly optimizes for these complementary tasks using a flexible, overlapping patch structure could better integrate local texture, global structure, and geometric relationships, leading to more robust and generalizable features.
proposals.hypothesis: Training a single Vision Transformer (ViT) model with a joint objective of spatial context prediction, colorization, and inpainting on a set of variably overlapping or nested image regions will force the model to develop a more unified and geometrically coherent understanding of visual scenes than training on these tasks separately or with fixed grids.
proposals.method: We will design a multi-task self-supervised framework where the input is a set of image regions sampled with random scales, aspect ratios, and significant overlap. A shared ViT backbone will process these regions, with task-specific heads. The spatial context objective will be a permutation prediction task on the overlapping patches' positional tokens, creating ambiguity that must be resolved using signals from the other tasks. The colorization and inpainting tasks will operate on masked versions of these same overlapping regions, providing the necessary semantic and textural context. Training will use a weighted sum of the three losses, with attention mechanisms facilitating cross-task and cross-patch information flow.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel self-supervised learning paradigm that explicitly leverages geometric ambiguity between overlapping contexts to drive robust feature learning. It will provide new insights into how multi-task objectives can be synergistically combined at the input level, and we expect the learned representations to show improved performance on downstream tasks requiring spatial reasoning, such as object detection and semantic segmentation.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the use of a flexible, overlapping patch structure as the common input for multiple self-supervised tasks, creating a deliberate ambiguity in spatial relationships that the model must resolve by integrating signals across tasks, moving beyond the standard fixed-grid tokenization of images.

proposals.title: Context is All You Need? Multi-Task Self-Supervision for Robust Visual Representation Learning
proposals.motivation: Self-supervised learning (SSL) for vision has advanced through diverse pretext tasks like jigsaw puzzles, colorization, and inpainting. However, each task captures only a specific facet of visual structure, potentially leading to representations that are brittle or biased. A unified framework that combines complementary tasks is needed to learn more general and robust visual features, moving beyond benchmarks to real-world scene understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that jointly training a vision transformer (ViT) on a combination of spatial context prediction (e.g., Jigsaw), colorization, and masked image inpainting will yield visual representations that are more semantically meaningful, transfer better to downstream tasks, and are more robust to occlusions and corruptions than those learned from any single task alone.
proposals.method: We will design a multi-head ViT encoder where a shared backbone processes image patches. The model will be trained concurrently on three pretext tasks: 1) Spatial context prediction via a jigsaw permutation head, 2) Colorization via a per-patch regression head in CIELAB space, and 3) Masked autoencoding (inpainting) via a decoder that reconstructs masked patches. A key challenge is balancing task gradients; we will experiment with uncertainty-based weighting and gradient surgery. Training will be performed on large-scale unlabeled datasets like ImageNet-1K.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic analysis of how complementary self-supervised tasks interact, potentially revealing synergistic effects. It will deliver a new state-of-the-art multi-task SSL model and benchmark its robustness on tasks like object detection, segmentation, and out-of-distribution generalization, pushing towards more holistic scene understanding.
proposals.novelty: While multi-task SSL is not new, the specific integration of high-level spatial reasoning (context) with low-level photometric (colorization) and generative (inpainting) tasks within a modern ViT architecture is unexplored. The novelty lies in the structured combination and the analysis of whether these tasks compete or cooperate for a shared representation.

proposals.title: Stratified Video Modeling: Self-Supervised Learning via Temporal Context Prediction
proposals.motivation: Current self-supervised video models often rely on contrastive learning or frame-level pretext tasks that may not explicitly enforce a deep understanding of temporal causality and lawful scene transitions. Learning robust temporal representations from unlabeled video is crucial for video understanding, prediction, and generation, yet remains a core challenge. Drawing an analogy to stratigraphy, where geologists infer missing layers from surrounding context, provides a novel conceptual framework for this problem.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a self-supervised task which forces a model to predict the content of a masked frame using only the temporal context of other frames will lead to video representations that better capture scene dynamics, object permanence, and causal temporal order, compared to existing methods.
proposals.method: We will adapt a Vision Transformer (ViT) architecture for video, treating frames as sequence tokens. During training, we will randomly mask a high proportion of frames (e.g., 80-90%) in a clip, leaving only a sparse, irregular 'stratigraphic' sample. The model, using a causal attention mask over time, must reconstruct the content of the masked frames at the pixel or feature level, conditioned only on the unmasked context. This extends the masked autoencoding principle from MAE to the temporal dimension with a geology-inspired masking strategy, forcing reliance on temporal, not just spatial, context.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel self-supervised objective for video based on temporal context prediction, 2) an analysis of the representations learned, showing improved performance on downstream tasks like action recognition and video prediction, and 3) a conceptual bridge between geological inference and computer vision, demonstrating the value of cross-disciplinary analogies for algorithm design.
proposals.novelty: While masked modeling exists for images and video, the key novelty is the stratigraphy-inspired formulation: predicting entire missing 'layers' (frames) from a sparse, irregular temporal context, which is distinct from predicting adjacent frames or next tokens in a dense sequence. This explicitly tests the model's ability to reason about longer-range temporal dependencies and scene state evolution.

proposals.title: Unsupervised Discovery of Object Parts and Fine Sub-Categories via Counterfactual Feature Perturbation
proposals.motivation: Modern self-supervised vision models learn powerful, holistic image representations but often lack explicit, structured understanding of object composition. This limits their utility for detailed scene analysis and downstream tasks requiring fine-grained distinctions without manual annotation. Building on the success of methods like DINO and iBOT, there is a clear opportunity to push beyond whole-object discovery towards unsupervised part-level and sub-category reasoning.
proposals.hypothesis: By systematically perturbing learned feature tokens in a pre-trained vision transformer and analyzing the induced changes in the representation space, we can induce a structured decomposition of objects into semantically meaningful parts and discover latent sub-categories within broader object classes.
proposals.method: We will leverage a pre-trained vision transformer (e.g., DINOv2) as a frozen feature backbone. Inspired by 'Visual Jenga', we will develop a counterfactual inpainting procedure at the feature-token level, masking and reconstructing subsets of tokens to measure their mutual dependencies and salience. We will then apply spectral clustering or a non-parametric Bayesian mixture model on the resulting token affinity graph to group tokens into coherent part segments. For sub-category discovery, we will perform hierarchical clustering on pooled part-level descriptors across the dataset.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, purely unsupervised algorithm for discovering object parts and fine sub-categories, validated on datasets like Pascal VOC and COCO. It will provide new insights into the compositional structure learned by self-supervised models and release a toolkit for fine-grained visual concept mining without labels.
proposals.novelty: While prior work discovers whole objects or scene-level structure, our approach explicitly targets the finer-grained, intra-object level by directly manipulating and analyzing the feature token graph, bridging the gap between holistic representation learning and detailed compositional understanding.

proposals.title: Visual Co-Evolution: Learning Global Patch Context via Contrastive Mutual Information Maximization
proposals.motivation: Current vision models, including Vision Transformers, primarily capture local relationships between neighboring patches via attention. However, many semantic and functional relationships in scenes are non-local (e.g., a keyboard and a monitor, a wheel and a road). We lack a principled, data-driven method to discover these long-range, consistent contextual associations from large-scale image data, which is crucial for robust scene understanding and generation.
proposals.hypothesis: By treating a diverse image dataset as an evolutionary family and image patches as residues, we can adapt principles from protein co-evolution analysis to learn an embedding space where patches with high statistical mutual information are proximate, thereby revealing globally consistent visual context.
proposals.method: We will segment images from a large dataset (e.g., LAION) into a grid of patches, forming visual 'sequences'. Inspired by Direct Coupling Analysis (DCA), we will estimate a patch-patch interaction matrix by computing pairwise mutual information across the dataset. We will then train a Vision Transformer encoder using a contrastive objective: for a given anchor patch, positive pairs are other patches with high estimated mutual information (strong co-variation), while negatives are randomly sampled patches. The model's attention mechanism will be adapted to weight these learned, global associations during inference.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, interpretable model of global visual context that captures semantic and functional object relationships beyond spatial proximity. It will enhance tasks like scene completion, counterfactual reasoning (extending Visual Jenga), and few-shot recognition by leveraging learned co-dependencies. The method offers a new perspective on visual data as an evolutionary system.
proposals.novelty: While contrastive learning is established, applying the formal co-evolution framework from computational biology to visual patch context is novel. It moves beyond modeling local attention to infer a global, statistical network of patch dependencies from population-level data, distinct from training-time register tokens or persistent 3D state.

proposals.title: JigsawGAN: Learning Abstract Relational Priors via Cross-Image Patch Assembly
proposals.motivation: Current vision models excel at recognizing and generating content from single images but struggle to learn and apply abstract, category-level structural relationships that generalize across instances. This limits their ability to reason about object geometry in a data-efficient, compositional way. We propose a novel task to explicitly learn these relational priors by forcing a model to synthesize coherent novel images from patches of different source images.
proposals.hypothesis: A model trained to solve a cross-image jigsaw puzzle, where patches from multiple semantically related images must be assembled into a novel, plausible output, will learn to encode and apply in-context, abstract relational constraints about object structure, moving beyond low-level texture matching.
proposals.method: We will develop a conditional GAN or diffusion framework where the generator receives a set of shuffled patches from N different images of the same category (e.g., car fronts) and a target layout. The model must output a coherent image adhering to that layout. A key component is a relational attention module that operates on the patch tokens, allowing the model to reason about geometric and semantic compatibility between patches from different sources. Training uses a combination of adversarial loss, a perceptual loss (e.g., VGG) to enforce semantic plausibility, and a puzzle-consistency loss.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a new self-supervised task for learning disentangled, transferable relational knowledge. It will provide a diagnostic tool for evaluating abstract reasoning in vision models and could yield improved priors for few-shot generation and counterfactual image editing, as explored in lines of work like Visual Jenga.
proposals.novelty: Unlike standard jigsaw puzzles that reassemble a single image, our method forces synthesis from a multi-image context, making memorization impossible and necessitating learning of category-level structural abstractions. It bridges the gap between discriminative jigsaw tasks and generative modeling of relational constraints.

proposals.title: Token-Scene Distillation: Lightweight Unsupervised Feature Learning for Mobile Vision via Attention-Consistent Pruning
proposals.motivation: Modern unsupervised visual feature learning models, particularly Vision Transformers (ViTs), are computationally expensive and memory-intensive, precluding their use on embedded or mobile devices where labeled data is scarce. While model compression techniques exist, they are typically designed for supervised tasks and degrade the rich, scene-level representations learned via modern pretext tasks like masked image modeling. There is a critical need for compression methods that preserve the scene understanding and token relationships crucial for downstream on-device applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a Vision Transformer's attention patterns, which encode critical scene structure and object dependencies, can be used as a persistent distillation target to guide the creation of a significantly smaller, attention-consistent student network capable of high-quality unsupervised feature learning.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage compression framework. First, we will train a large teacher ViT (e.g., MAE or iBOT) on a standard dataset. Second, we will train a compact student ViT from scratch using a novel distillation loss that combines the standard reconstruction loss from masked image modeling with a novel attention consistency loss, forcing the student's cross-attention maps between visible and masked tokens to match the teacher's. We will integrate this with structured pruning of attention heads and MLP blocks, guided by the variance in their attention patterns, to create an ultra-lightweight architecture suitable for mobile deployment.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical method for creating high-performance, unsupervised visual feature learners that run efficiently on mobile hardware. It will contribute a new theoretical perspective on compressing self-supervised models by showing that preserving relational attention structure is more critical than mimicking final embeddings. We will release benchmark results for on-device linear probing and fine-tuning on tasks like mobile visual recognition.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior compression work focused on supervised logit matching or feature regression, our method directly distills the internal attention mechanisms that underpin scene understanding in unsupervised ViTs. This approach is novel in leveraging the 'persistent state' of attention, as explored in Efros's prior work, as the primary target for model miniaturization.

proposals.title: Multi-Scale Contextual Jenga: Learning Cross-Magnification Dependencies with Vision Transformers
proposals.motivation: Current vision models, including Vision Transformers (ViTs), primarily learn context between tokens from a single, fixed image scale. This limits their ability to understand the hierarchical dependencies between fine-grained details and broader scene semantics, a capability crucial for tasks like fine-grained classification, out-of-context detection, and robust image inpainting. Pre-2026 work, such as Visual Jenga, explores object dependencies but remains within a single resolution.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling attention between image patches sampled at different magnification levels within a single transformer architecture will lead to richer, more robust scene representations than learning context at a single scale alone.
proposals.method: We will extend a ViT backbone with a multi-scale patch embedding layer that extracts tokens from the same image region at multiple resolutions (e.g., 1x, 2x, 4x zoom). A key innovation is a cross-scale attention module where the query for a fine-grained token can attend to keys/values from coarser-scale tokens from a wider surrounding area, and vice-versa. This architecture will be trained with a joint objective combining standard image classification and a novel counterfactual inpainting task inspired by Visual Jenga, but where the missing region and its context are defined across different scales.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a new architectural paradigm for learning multi-scale contextual relationships in a single forward pass. It will provide empirical evidence on benchmark datasets that cross-scale attention improves model interpretability (via attention maps) and robustness to scale-varying corruptions. The method will also offer a new tool for analyzing scene structure across resolutions.
proposals.novelty: While multi-scale features are common in CNNs and some hierarchical ViTs, the explicit, simultaneous learning of attention *between* different magnification levels within a transformer's persistent state is novel. It moves beyond simple feature pyramid fusion to model direct, learnable dependencies between semantic concepts at different granularities.

proposals.title: Volumetric Jigsaw: Unsupervised 3D Representation Learning via Spatial Context Prediction in Medical Image Stacks
proposals.motivation: Unsupervised representation learning for 3D medical data (e.g., CT, MRI) is critically important due to the scarcity of expert annotations. Current 2D pretext tasks fail to capture the essential 3D anatomical context and structural dependencies inherent in volumetric data. Extending spatial context prediction frameworks, which have proven powerful for 2D images, to 3D could unlock more semantically meaningful and transferable features for downstream tasks like segmentation and diagnosis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a 3D Vision Transformer, trained to predict the spatial arrangement of permuted 3D tokens from medical volumes, will learn rich, transferable representations of anatomical structure and inter-organ dependencies without manual supervision.
proposals.method: We will extend the 2D Jigsaw pretext task to 3D by dividing input volumes into a grid of 3D cuboid tokens and applying random 3D permutations. A 3D Vision Transformer (ViT), adapted to process the tokenized volume, will be trained to regress the original 3D spatial coordinates of each permuted token using a cross-attention mechanism over the scrambled sequence. Inspired by 'Visual Jenga', we will also explore a counterfactual variant where a subset of tokens is masked (simulating missing anatomy) to force the model to infer deeper structural dependencies.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, scalable method for unsupervised feature learning from unlabeled 3D medical datasets. It will demonstrate the transferability of learned features to data-scarce supervised tasks like organ segmentation and anomaly detection. The project will also yield insights into how 3D structural priors are captured by transformer architectures.
proposals.novelty: While 3D autoencoders and contrastive learning exist, applying a spatial context prediction (Jigsaw) objective directly to 3D transformer tokens for medical data is unexplored. The integration of counterfactual masking within this 3D framework, drawing from 'Visual Jenga', is a novel approach to learning object dependencies in volumetric space.

proposals.title: Inverse Image Editing: Inferring User Intent from Image Pairs via Latent Constraint Diffusion
proposals.motivation: Image editing tools powered by diffusion models or GANs allow users to transform images via scribbles, text, or drag-and-drop. However, these systems operate in a forward direction: user input produces an edit. The inverse problem—inferring the likely user constraints that explain a given 'before and after' image pair—is unsolved. This capability is crucial for understanding user behavior, building better interactive systems, and learning editing priors from large-scale image pair datasets (e.g., time-lapse photos or historical edits).
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the latent space of a pre-trained text-to-image diffusion model, combined with a learned attention mechanism over image tokens, can be inverted to recover a sparse, interpretable set of user constraints (e.g., stroke maps, semantic masks, or attribute changes) that maximally explain the transformation between two images.
proposals.method: We will train a transformer-based inference network that takes a concatenated 'before' and 'after' image pair as input. The network will use a cross-attention mechanism between tokens from both images to identify salient, changed regions. It will output a probabilistic map of likely user scribbles or masks. This will be trained using a synthetic dataset generated by applying randomized edits (via models like InstructPix2Pix or Stable Diffusion inpainting) to images, where the 'ground truth' user constraints are known. A key component is a diffusion-based consistency loss that ensures the predicted constraints, when fed back into a frozen editing model, reconstruct the 'after' image.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce the first framework for inferring editing intent from image pairs, formalizing a novel inverse problem in computer vision. It will yield a model that can decompose complex image transformations into a minimal set of user actions, providing a tool for analyzing editing workflows. The method could also enable new applications in tutorial generation, edit propagation, and dataset curation for interactive AI.
proposals.novelty: While prior work (like Efros's 'Visual Jenga') studies object dependencies via counterfactuals, and vision transformers analyze token relationships, the explicit inversion of the editing process to recover user-specific constraints from only the output image pair is a novel and non-obvious direction. It flips the standard paradigm of generative models from 'constraints to image' to 'image pair to constraints'.

ideas.title: Predict context across different images
ideas.description: Challenge the within-image assumption by predicting spatial relationships between patches from different but semantically related images.
ideas.sourceTitle: Unsupervised Visual Representation Learning by Context Prediction

ideas.title: Cross-Image Semantic Jigsaw for Abstract Relational Context Learning
ideas.description: This approach creates a jigsaw task where the patches are drawn from multiple different source images belonging to the same semantic category (e.g., various faces, different cars). The model must reassemble them into a single, novel, and semantically plausible output image, not a memorized original. This forces the model to learn and apply in-context, abstract relational constraints about object structure and category-level geometry, moving beyond low-level feature matching. Success is measured by the structural coherence and semantic plausibility of the generated assembly, evaluated by a separate pre-trained vision model.

ideas.title: Extend to video inpainting and feature learning
ideas.description: Apply the context encoder framework to video data for spatiotemporal feature learning.
ideas.sourceTitle: Context Encoders: Feature Learning by Inpainting

ideas.title: Unsupervised Feature-Aware Data Synthesis for Compositional Discovery
ideas.description: This research direction proposes using a pixel-level translator not for generic data augmentation, but to synthesize novel visual samples that systematically vary and recombine features identified as salient by an unsupervised representation learner. The goal is to generate 'feature probes'—images designed to test and expand the model's understanding of object parts, sub-categories, and their compositional rules. By analyzing the model's consistency and failures on these synthetic probes, we can iteratively refine both the discovery algorithm and the data synthesis process. This creates a synergistic, self-improving loop for compositional visual understanding without manual labels.

ideas.title: Combine context prediction with other self-supervised tasks
ideas.description: Integrate the spatial context task with other unsupervised objectives like colorization or inpainting for a more robust representation.

ideas.title: Challenge that ImageNet features are optimal

ideas.sourceTitle: The Unreasonable Effectiveness of Deep Features as a Perceptual Metric

ideas.title: Multi-Task Self-Supervision with Adaptive, Overlapping Context Patches
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a self-supervised learning framework that trains a model using a joint objective combining spatial context prediction, image colorization, and inpainting. Crucially, the input is not a fixed grid of distinct patches, but a set of variably overlapping or even nested image regions. The synergy emerges because the overlapping patches provide ambiguous and multi-scale context for the spatial arrangement task, while the colorization and inpainting tasks provide the necessary semantic and textural signals to disambiguate relationships between overlapping areas. This forces the model to develop a unified understanding of both local texture, global structure, and the geometric relationships between different spatial scales within the same image, leading to more robust visual features.

ideas.title: Integrate with other unsupervised representation learning methods

ideas.title: Cross-Image Context Prediction via Cross-Document Coreference Resolution
ideas.description: This research challenges the within-image assumption in computer vision by predicting spatial relationships between patches from different but semantically related images. The analogy is drawn from cross-document coreference resolution in NLP, where entities are linked across documents using shared context. The adapted solution involves training a model to treat patches as visual mentions, learning to project them into a shared latent space, and predicting geometric relationships (like 'above' or 'adjacent to') between patches across image boundaries, thereby building a cross-image understanding of spatial context.

ideas.title: Temporal Context Prediction via Stratigraphic Sequencing Analogy
ideas.description: This idea extends the patch context prediction framework to video by drawing an analogy from stratigraphy in geology. In stratigraphy, the sequence of sedimentary layers reveals temporal history, and missing layers are inferred from the context of surrounding strata. Analogously, we propose a self-supervised learning task for video where a model must predict the content of a masked frame (or spatio-temporal patch) based solely on the context provided by other, unmasked frames in the sequence. This forces the model to learn robust representations of temporal ordering, scene dynamics, and lawful transitions, much like a geologist reconstructs past depositional environments from partial stratigraphic records. The solution adapts the core principle of using contextual sequence to predict missing elements, transferring it from the spatial-depth domain of rock layers to the spatio-temporal domain of video frames.

ideas.title: Cross-Modal Domain Adaptation via Spectral Alignment for Audio and Text
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea of applying cycle-consistent adversarial adaptation to non-visual data by drawing an analogy from geophysics. In seismic signal processing, a proven method for cross-domain adaptation involves spectral alignment—transforming the frequency-domain representation of signals from one geological survey domain to match another. We adapt this solution to the machine learning context for audio and text data. The proposed research will test a cycle-consistent adversarial framework where the adaptation occurs not on raw data but on their core representational distributions: the frequency spectra (e.g., mel-spectrograms) for audio signals and the latent embedding/attention distributions for textual sequences. The goal is to learn bidirectional mappings that preserve content (e.g., phonetic information in audio, semantic meaning in text) while aligning the feature-space distributions across domains, such as from clean to noisy audio or from one textual genre to another, thereby enabling effective unsupervised domain adaptation for these non-visual modalities.

ideas.title: Feature Alignment Stabilized by Pixel-Space Consistency
ideas.description: This approach maintains the core simplicity of feature-level alignment for domain adaptation but guards against its latent failure modes. A minimal auxiliary network enforces that the adapted features can be mapped back to a pixel-space representation consistent with the source domain's data manifold. This pixel-space consistency signal acts as a regularizer, preventing the feature adapter from achieving alignment by discarding or distorting essential semantic content, thereby improving robustness without relying on complex pixel-level transformations.

ideas.title: Adapting Protein Co-Evolution Analysis to Visual Patch Context Modeling
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from computational biology's protein co-evolution analysis to the problem of modeling patch context in images. In protein science, Direct Coupling Analysis (DCA) infers structural and functional relationships between amino acids by analyzing their co-evolution patterns across a large family of related protein sequences. Analogously, we propose treating a massive, diverse image dataset as a 'family' of visual 'sequences'. Each image is segmented into patches (analogous to residues), and a contrastive learning framework is designed to identify patches that consistently co-vary across the dataset, indicating a strong semantic or structural relationship. The training objective would contrast pairs of patches with high mutual information (positive) against random patch pairs (negative), learning an embedding space where semantically linked visual concepts are proximate, thereby capturing a rich, data-driven model of visual context that extends beyond immediate neighbors to global, functional associations.

ideas.title: Source-Free Multi-Modal/Multi-Task Adaptation via Cross-Modal Knowledge Consolidation
ideas.description: This research investigates how to adapt a pre-trained multi-modal or multi-task model to a new target domain after source data access has been revoked. The core challenge is to prevent the degradation of cross-modal alignments and task interdependencies during adaptation when only unlabeled or partially labeled target data is available. The novel direction explores using the structural relationships between modalities (e.g., vision-language correspondences) or between tasks as a self-supervisory signal to constrain and guide the adaptation process, effectively 'distilling' and preserving the original model's relational knowledge. This enables robust, efficient adaptation for complex models in data-constrained or privacy-sensitive scenarios where source data cannot be retained.

ideas.title: Invert the domain translation directionality

ideas.title: Feature-Level Domain Adaptation via Statistical Moment Matching
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that robust domain adaptation requires complex, pixel-level transformations or adversarial alignment at every network layer. Drawing an analogy from statistical thermodynamics, where macroscopic system properties are predicted by matching the statistical moments of particle distributions rather than simulating each particle, we propose adapting models by aligning the statistical moments of feature distributions. Specifically, during adaptation to a new target domain, we minimize the difference between the mean and covariance of feature activations from the source and target domains at a critical, high-level layer of a neural network. This feature-level statistical moment matching simplifies the adaptation process, avoids costly pixel-space manipulations, and provides a stable, distributional constraint for robust performance across domains.

ideas.title: Unsupervised Discovery of Semantic Sub-units via Spectral Graph Partitioning of Feature Affinities
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from computational chemistry for identifying functional subunits in complex molecules to the problem of finer-grained unsupervised discovery in computer vision. In the analogous field, the correlation matrix of atomic motions is analyzed using spectral graph partitioning to discover dynamically independent protein domains without labels. The adapted research idea applies this by using the learned features from a pre-trained model to construct a feature affinity graph across an unlabeled image dataset, where nodes represent spatial feature vectors and edge weights represent their co-activation or statistical correlation. Applying spectral clustering or community detection algorithms to this graph can then partition the feature space into semantically coherent groups, autonomously discovering object parts or latent sub-categories as stable communities within the representation, enabling a data-driven 'parsing' of visual concepts.

ideas.title: Use Immunological Negative Selection for Feature Invariance
ideas.description: This idea adapts the negative selection algorithm from computational immunology to improve contrastive learning for domain-invariant features. In immunology, detectors are censored if they recognize 'self' to prevent autoimmunity, ensuring they only respond to foreign 'non-self' patterns. Adapted here, we define the set of features common across all domains (the invariant core) as 'self'. During model training, we employ a contrastive loss framework that actively repels (negatively selects) feature embeddings that are distinctive to any single domain, treating them as detrimental 'non-self' variations. Simultaneously, the loss attracts embeddings from different domains that correspond to the same semantic content, reinforcing the 'self' invariant features. This results in a feature representation where domain-specific signals are systematically suppressed, leading to more robust and generalizable domain-invariant features.

ideas.title: Dual application: unsupervised domain discovery

ideas.title: Source-Free Adaptation Anchored by a Generative Source Prior
ideas.description: This method enables robust adaptation after source data deletion by first distilling the source data distribution into a compact, frozen generative model. During adaptation to the target domain, this generative prior acts as a regularizer, constraining the model's feature space to remain consistent with the source manifold and enabling synthetic feature replay. This approach mitigates catastrophic forgetting and domain drift, preserving the model's original generalization power. The system maintains data privacy as the generative prior does not allow exact reconstruction of source samples, fulfilling the source-free constraint while being more stable than direct inversion.

ideas.title: Improve training stability

ideas.title: Decoupled Style-Specific Distillation for Robust On-Device Adaptation
ideas.description: This system separates the heavy lifting of domain adaptation from the mobile deployment target. A central teacher model, employing a full adversarial adaptation framework, learns to transform data from a source domain to match the statistical 'style' of a target device class (e.g., a specific smartphone camera). It then acts as a fixed style expert, distilling its adapted representation into a purpose-built, tiny student network via knowledge distillation focused on style-consistent features and output distributions. The resulting student model is lightweight enough for on-device inference and is robust because it encapsulates a pre-computed, strong adaptation to the target domain's style, without requiring adversarial training on the device itself.

ideas.title: Curriculum Learning for Gradual Adaptation Across Evolving Label Spaces
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a curriculum learning framework specifically designed for adaptation scenarios where the source and target label spaces are different or only partially overlapping. The curriculum is structured to first adapt on easy, shared sub-tasks or a coarser label taxonomy, then gradually introduce the novel target classes and more complex discrimination tasks. This allows the model to leverage stable, transferable features from the source domain before tackling the harder problem of discriminating new categories, mitigating negative transfer and catastrophic forgetting. The core novelty is in defining the 'easy-to-hard' sequence based on the semantic and structural relationships between the source and target output spaces, rather than assuming a fixed, shared set of classes.

ideas.title: Optimal Transport for Adaptive Patch Coupling in Domain Alignment
ideas.description: This research direction fuses optimal transport (OT) for domain mapping with a flexible, non-rigid patch structure. Instead of using a fixed grid of patches, the method employs OT to compute an adaptive coupling between source and target domains at multiple scales, where the 'units' of transport can be variable-sized, overlapping, or non-adjacent image regions. The OT cost function is extended to include a penalty for the geometric and semantic distortion induced by different patch configurations, jointly solving for the optimal patch definition and the alignment map. This allows the model to dynamically group pixels into semantically coherent 'super-patches' for transport where domains are structurally dissimilar, leading to more robust and interpretable domain adaptation, especially for tasks with significant geometric or topological shifts.

ideas.title: Adaptive Gating for Consistent Multi-Task Self-Supervised Learning
ideas.description: This approach integrates multiple self-supervised tasks but introduces a meta-learning controller that continuously evaluates task interactions. It employs a gating mechanism to adaptively blend or select task-specific gradients based on real-time measurements of task alignment and their contribution to a robust, generalizing feature space. This ensures the model focuses on synergistic task combinations, avoiding representation degradation from conflicting objectives. The result is a more reliably robust representation than a static combination of tasks can provide.

ideas.title: Use jigsaw puzzle solving as task

ideas.title: High-Resolution Domain Adaptation via Hierarchical Feature Space Remapping
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel adaptation framework designed for ultra-high-resolution domains like digital pathology and radiology. It completely avoids computationally prohibitive pixel-level transformations by operating solely on hierarchical, patch-based feature representations extracted from a pre-trained encoder. The core innovation is a multi-tiered alignment mechanism that matches feature distributions at progressively coarser semantic scales—from local cellular structures to global tissue organization—ensuring robust domain invariance. This synergy allows the model to handle the massive data footprint of medical scans while simplifying the adaptation pipeline and improving generalization to unseen high-resolution target domains.

ideas.title: Explore higher resolution image synthesis

ideas.title: Cross-Domain Taxonomic Alignment via Partial Correspondence
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that source and target tasks must share a label space by drawing an analogy from bioinformatics and comparative genomics. In that field, aligning gene sets across species does not assume a universal gene catalog; instead, tools like orthology detection find correspondences (orthologs, paralogs) and account for unique genes. The adapted solution is to develop a machine learning method that, instead of requiring a fixed shared label space, learns a flexible, probabilistic alignment between the output classes of source and target tasks based on the model's feature representations. It would classify relationships between labels as directly transferable, related but distinct, or novel, enabling effective adaptation and knowledge transfer for tasks with different or only partially overlapping output classes.

ideas.title: Predict absolute image coordinates via celestial navigation principles
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that positional understanding in vision models must be learned through relative relationships (e.g., attention between patches). By analogy to celestial navigation, where absolute position is deduced from local observations of a global, structured sky, we invert the task. The model is trained to predict a patch's absolute coordinate grid location from its content alone. The analogous solution—using angular measurements to known anchors—is adapted by having the model learn a set of implicit 'visual anchor features' (analogous to stars). From a patch's content, it infers its position by relating its features to this fixed, globally consistent anchor system, establishing an absolute coordinate prediction directly.

ideas.title: Synthetic Data Augmentation via Immunological Affinity Maturation
ideas.description: This idea expands the dual-use of a pixel-level translator for data augmentation by drawing an analogy from computational immunology. The analogous solution from in silico vaccine design involves generating a broad library of synthetic antigen variants and using a predictive model to select only the most immunogenic candidates for further development. Adapted to machine learning, the pixel-level translator generates a diverse set of synthetic training samples. A lightweight utility predictor, acting as a 'critic', then evaluates and selects only the synthetic samples predicted to be most beneficial for model robustness—such as those on the current decision boundary or in underrepresented feature regions—for inclusion in the training data. This creates a closed-loop, efficient augmentation system that prioritizes quality and impact over sheer quantity, scaling the utility of the generated data.

ideas.title: Contrastive Continual Alignment for Evolving Domains
ideas.description: This research direction investigates a continual domain adaptation paradigm where a model must adapt sequentially to a target domain that evolves continuously over time, such as a sensor's degradation or shifting user behavior. The core innovation is the use of contrastive learning objectives to explicitly shape the feature space, pulling together representations of semantically similar data across different temporal slices of the evolving domain while pushing apart dissimilar ones. This creates a stable, temporally coherent feature manifold that anchors the adaptation process, allowing the model to track the domain drift smoothly. Consequently, the approach mitigates the compounding error and catastrophic forgetting typical in sequential adaptation by leveraging contrastive signals to enforce consistency and gradual transformation in the learned features.

ideas.title: Modeling Long-Range Spatial Dependencies via Graph Neural Networks with Multi-Hop Attention
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from computational chemistry to explore complex spatial relationships in visual data. In molecular modeling, Graph Neural Networks with multi-hop message passing capture long-range atomic interactions beyond direct bonds. By representing an image's patches as nodes in a graph and using a GNN with multi-hop attention, we can investigate predicting relationships for longer-range or multi-patch configurations. This approach allows the model to learn dependencies and configurations that span across non-adjacent patches, moving beyond the limitation of only considering the eight immediate neighbors.

ideas.title: Causally-Constrained Cycle-Consistent Domain Generation
ideas.description: This system generates realistic source domain data from target features by enforcing causal and structural integrity. It incorporates a domain-specific causal graph to constrain the generative model, ensuring outputs obey the underlying physical or logical relationships of the source. A cycle-consistency loss is applied, requiring that translating the generated source data back to the target domain reconstructs the original input features. This results in synthetic source data that is not only statistically realistic but also semantically faithful and functionally valid for downstream tasks.

ideas.title: Use variational autoencoders for multi-modality
ideas.description: Employ a variational framework to explicitly model the distribution of possible completions.

ideas.title: Task-Relaxed Adaptation with Open and Evolving Label Spaces
ideas.description: This research direction develops adaptation frameworks that simultaneously relax two core assumptions: the need for a strict cycle-consistency constraint and the requirement of a shared, predefined label space. It investigates methods, such as relaxed optimal transport or contrastive alignment with uncertainty, that can discover partial or hierarchical correspondences between the source and target tasks' outputs, which may be disjoint, overlapping, or at different granularities. The goal is to enable practical adaptation in scenarios where tasks are related but not identical, and where strong supervisory signals like paired data cycles are unavailable, focusing on learning a soft, probabilistic mapping between task structures instead of enforcing a bijective transformation.

ideas.title: Geophysical Inversion for Synthetic Domain Generation
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to the inverse problem from geophysical seismic inversion to the context of domain generation. In geophysics, observed seismic data (the target) is used to infer subsurface rock properties (the source) by iteratively refining a source model through a physical forward model. Here, we analogously use the adapted model as a differentiable forward model. The goal is to generate realistic source data from target domain features by framing it as an inversion problem: optimize the parameters of a generative source model so that when its outputs are processed by the adapted model, they statistically match the given features of the target domain. This enables the scalable synthesis of source-domain data that is consistent with observed target-domain characteristics.

ideas.title: Improve robustness to varied input conditions

ideas.sourceTitle: Image-to-Image Translation with Conditional Adversarial Networks

ideas.title: Apply Optimal Transport with Geodesic Flow for Domain Mapping
ideas.description: This idea expands the original application of optimal transport for domain mapping by drawing an analogy from fluid dynamics and meteorology. In those fields, mapping between two states of a fluid system (e.g., weather patterns) is often achieved by computing a geodesic—the path of minimal energy—on the manifold of possible configurations. Adapting this solution, we propose to guide pixel and feature space alignment in domain adaptation by computing a geodesic flow between the source and target domain distributions in Wasserstein space. This approach frames the optimal transport problem as finding a continuous, smooth trajectory of intermediate domains, defined by a Riemannian metric on the probability manifold. This can lead to more stable and physically-inspired alignment, mitigating abrupt transitions and potentially improving generalization for the target task.

ideas.title: Efficient Multi-Resolution Curriculum Learning for High-Resolution Medical Image Adaptation
ideas.description: This redesigned framework overcomes the computational intractability of high-resolution medical images by employing a curriculum learning approach that strategically scales resolution and patch complexity. It uses a lightweight selector network to identify and prioritize diagnostically relevant regions for high-resolution processing, while efficiently handling background context at lower resolutions. The system dynamically manages memory by caching only essential intermediate features, enabling training and inference on standard clinical hardware. This results in a practical, deployable adaptation framework that maintains diagnostic accuracy without unsustainable resource demands.

ideas.title: Anomaly Detection via Predictive Coding Mismatch, Inspired by Immunological Memory
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from immunology. The adaptive immune system solves the analogous problem of detecting novel pathogens by learning from failures to recognize 'self' through a process called negative selection. We adapt this solution to the research context of context prediction in machine learning. The adapted research idea involves training a system not only to perform context prediction but to meta-learn from its failed predictions. These failures are systematically collected and used to build an 'anomaly memory'—a secondary model explicitly tuned to the features and patterns that caused the primary model's predictive breakdown. This creates a robust anomaly detection mechanism where deviations from the learned contextual model are not just errors but informative signals that refine the system's sensitivity to novel objects or anomalous scenarios, effectively challenging the assumption that prediction failures are merely noise.

ideas.title: Apply curriculum learning strategies

ideas.title: Adaptive Domain Bridging via Intermediate Ecological Niches
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from conservation biology's solution for translocating species across large environmental gaps, this research idea proposes to enhance model robustness for extreme domain shifts by constructing a bridge of intermediate domains. In conservation, 'stepping-stone' habitats are used to allow gradual acclimatization. Adapted to machine learning, the solution involves algorithmically generating or curating a sequence of data domains that form a smooth trajectory from the source to the target. The model is then trained progressively along this bridge via curriculum learning, enabling gradual adaptation of its internal representations. This method aims to mitigate the sharp performance drop typically seen in direct adaptation across large domain gaps by decomposing the challenging shift into a series of smaller, manageable ones.

ideas.title: Multi-Domain Adaptation via Federated Aggregation
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that domain adaptation frameworks must be limited to a single source or target domain. Drawing an analogy from Federated Learning, where a global model is trained by aggregating updates from multiple decentralized data sources, we propose a multi-domain adaptation framework using federated aggregation. The analogous solution involves techniques like Federated Averaging (FedAvg) to handle statistical heterogeneity across clients. Adapted to domain adaptation, multiple source domains (or target domains) are treated as distinct 'clients'. Each source domain trains a local feature extractor or adapter, and a central aggregation mechanism (e.g., weighted averaging of model parameters, gradients, or feature representations) combines them to learn a robust, domain-invariant model. This enables simultaneous learning from multiple source distributions and adaptation to one or more target domains, improving robustness and knowledge transfer.

ideas.title: Challenge the need for task-specific architectures

ideas.sourceTitle: Image-to-Image Translation with Conditional Adversarial Networks

ideas.title: Adapting Fragment Assembly Algorithms from Bioinformatics for Context Learning
ideas.description: This idea adapts solutions from bioinformatics fragment assembly—specifically the overlap-layout-consensus paradigm used in genome sequencing—to the method-transfer problem of using jigsaw puzzle solving as a context learning task. In genomics, short, shuffled DNA reads are assembled by finding overlapping sequences, building a connection graph, and finding a consensus path. Analogously, shuffled image patches can be treated as visual 'reads.' The adapted research idea involves developing a model that identifies visual or semantic compatibilities (overlaps) between patches, constructs a graph of potential adjacencies, and then employs a consensus optimization algorithm to reconstruct the most probable original image configuration. This frames context learning as a probabilistic graph-based reassembly problem, directly parallel to genomic sequence assembly.

ideas.title: Integrate a 'Primer Dose' of Labeled Data with Unlabeled Adaptation
ideas.description: This idea expands on integrating few-shot learning with a domain adaptation framework by drawing an analogy from immunology's prime-boost vaccination strategy. The analogous solution uses a minimal initial antigen dose to prime the immune system, followed by a boosting dose for robust immunity. Adapted to machine learning, the few labeled target-domain examples act as the 'prime,' providing a critical, minimal signal to correctly bias the model's feature alignment or adaptation direction. This primed model then undergoes a 'boost' phase via semi-supervised techniques (e.g., pseudo-labeling, consistency regularization) on the vast unlabeled target data, where the initial priming prevents divergence and dramatically improves the sample efficiency and final accuracy of the adaptation process.

ideas.title: Conflict-Aware Multi-Domain Adaptation via Graph-Guided Spectral Routing
ideas.description: This framework extends single-source/target adaptation to multi-domain settings by explicitly modeling inter-domain relationships. A meta-learned, soft domain affinity graph governs a gated routing network, dynamically filtering and blending features to promote positive transfer and suppress negative interference. Spectral analysis of domain feature spaces identifies conflicting components, which are then regularized during adaptation. This results in a system that robustly handles multiple, potentially adversarial domains without performance collapse on any individual domain.

ideas.title: Dynamic Relevance-Guided Prediction of Sparse Long-Range Patch Relationships
ideas.description: This research investigates predicting spatial relationships by dynamically identifying which longer-range interactions are contextually meaningful, avoiding a brute-force search over all possible configurations. A lightweight proposal module analyzes patch features to generate a sparse set of candidate distant patches likely to be relevant to a central query patch, based on learned measures of semantic and geometric affinity. The core relationship predictor then evaluates only these proposed candidate pairs, enabling efficient modeling of complex, non-local dependencies. This approach ensures scalability and focuses capacity on learning generally applicable structural priors rather than memorizing an exponential set of configurations.

ideas.title: Multi-Scale Patchwork Integration for Gigapixel Image Adaptation
ideas.description: This idea expands the original high-resolution image adaptation concept by drawing an analogy from astronomy, where massive, high-resolution sky survey images are processed. The analogous solution involves a hierarchical tiling (or 'bricking') strategy, where the large image is broken into manageable, overlapping segments for localized analysis, followed by a global co-addition process to ensure seamless, consistent integration of results across the entire field. Adapted to the medical imaging context, the research would develop a framework that decomposes ultra-high-resolution scans (e.g., whole-slide images in pathology) into a patchwork of tiles. A dual-scale adaptation process would then occur: a local model adapts to features within each tile, while a lightweight global model or set of constraints ensures anatomical, textural, and statistical consistency across tile boundaries. This approach makes scaling to arbitrarily high resolutions computationally feasible without losing the global context critical for accurate medical analysis.

ideas.title: Meta-Curriculum Learning for Adaptive Domain Shift Scheduling
ideas.description: This system automates the generation of an optimal adaptation curriculum by meta-learning a scheduling policy. It continuously evaluates the model's performance and the statistical properties of candidate source or intermediate domains to dynamically sequence the training process. The approach identifies which shifts are most beneficial at each learning stage, preventing suboptimal fixed orderings and adapting the curriculum to the model's current state. This results in more efficient and robust gradual adaptation without relying on handcrafted difficulty heuristics.

ideas.title: Adaptive Sparse Patch Selection with Learned Redundancy Control
ideas.description: This system replaces naive overlap or merging with a principled, learnable module that decides which patches to process. A small policy network, trained with reinforcement learning or gradient estimation, evaluates the potential information gain of sampling a patch at a given location and stride, actively minimizing redundancy. Overlapping regions from selected patches are fused via a cross-patch attention layer that resolves conflicts and consolidates features. This approach maintains the benefits of flexible patch coverage while guaranteeing computational efficiency and robust, non-redundant feature learning.

ideas.title: Incorporate geometric consistency from SfM
ideas.description: Use geometric verification from structure-from-motion to create a more robust spatial context signal.

ideas.title: Source-Free Adaptation via Epigenetic Memory Mechanisms
ideas.description: This idea inverts the source-free adaptation assumption by drawing an analogy from epigenetics. In developmental biology, cells adapt to environmental cues by laying down epigenetic markers that form a persistent, functional memory, allowing stable phenotypic changes without changing the underlying genetic code (the source). The analogous solution is to design a neural network training regimen where the initial exposure to source data induces not just weights, but a separate, modifiable 'epigenetic' parameter layer or a set of feature-space priors that captures the source domain's essential structure. During target domain adaptation, this epigenetic memory is consulted and updated to constrain and guide the plasticity of the main network weights, enabling effective, principled adaptation long after the source data has been discarded. This provides a mechanistic, memory-based alternative to conventional source-free methods that often rely on indirect statistical matching or pseudo-labeling.

ideas.title: Robust and Adaptive Optimal Transport for Domain Alignment via Outlier-Resistant Coupling
ideas.description: This approach enhances domain mapping by employing robust optimal transport formulations that are intrinsically resistant to outliers and noise. It dynamically computes a soft, adaptive coupling between domains, allowing for partial mass transfer and discarding of spurious correspondences. An auxiliary network learns to weight samples based on their alignment coherence, iteratively refining the transport plan. This results in a stable and principled alignment focused on the shared latent structure, improving generalization for downstream tasks.

ideas.title: Apply information-theoretic image measures

ideas.title: Shared Substrate Learning for Multi-Modal Models
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from systems biology—the design of a shared metabolic chassis for multi-product biosynthesis—to the problem of multi-modal, multi-task model adaptation. In metabolic engineering, a core set of pathways efficiently converts varied feedstocks into common precursors, which are then diverted to specialized branches for different products. The adapted idea is to design neural architectures that learn a unified, foundational 'substrate' representation from all input modalities. This substrate acts as a common, efficiently encoded intermediate. From this shared substrate, lightweight, task-specific 'specialist' modules branch out to produce simultaneous predictions. The research would focus on novel architectures and training regimes to learn this substrate, alongside gating or attention-based mechanisms to dynamically route information and manage interference between the competing downstream tasks, thereby improving efficiency and performance in multi-modal, multi-task settings.
